refined_solution:
## Original Problem

```text
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
```

## Problem Solved

I prove the following self-contained statement, which is slightly stronger than the final sentence of the original problem. Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers, let \(\mathbb R\) be the real numbers, let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), let \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and for \(\beta>0\) let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\). A clean triangle means the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). A clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) means a countable locally finite collection of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\). It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) sends triangles of the triangulation to triangles of the triangulation. A squeeze map on a clean triangle means an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there are a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation such that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle and such that, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\), \(f(p)=f(q)\) if and only if \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). The theorem proved below is that the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\). Consequently its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This strengthens the original problem, which asked only for the infimum.

## Proof

### Part I. The lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume throughout Part I that \(\beta\) is realized by a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\).

#### 1. The quotient is an embedded finite PL Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
Equivalently, \(M_\beta\) is obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus \(M_\beta\) is a compact Möbius band, and its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The realization condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\). Hence \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by hypothesis \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Therefore \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We next record that the quotient triangulation is finite. For \(k\ne0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) by Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus every nontrivial deck transformation has horizontal displacement at least \(\beta\), except even powers which have displacement at least \(2\beta\).

Let \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\). If the affine map \(f|_\Delta\) were not injective, then its linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\), impossible when \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f|_\Delta\) is injective on every triangle.

Consequently no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), and so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Since \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting the freeness of the deck action unless \(k=0\).

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles, by local finiteness. Therefore \(\mathcal T\) has only finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of triangles. Thus the quotient gives a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\). If one wants a literal simplicial complex, one may take its barycentric subdivision. On each original triangle \(F\) is affine and injective.

#### 2. The squeeze-cevian lemma

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(0\le s\le1\), set
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1.
\]
Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two non-boundary edges are strictly lengthened, so
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Because \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
since the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, and hence
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Therefore every segment from the apex \(a\) to the boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(f\).

In particular, every leaf segment defined below has nonzero image length. Also, because \(F\) is affine and strictly contracting on every boundary edge, every nontrivial boundary subarc of \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened in path length.

#### 3. The apex-to-boundary leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider all segments from the apex to the opposite boundary edge. These segments form a one-parameter family, naturally an interval. The endpoints of this interval correspond to the two non-boundary sides of the triangle.

Construct a graph \(\Lambda_{M}\) as follows. For every quotient triangle, take one interval of such apex-to-boundary cevians. Whenever two quotient triangles share a non-boundary edge, glue the corresponding endpoints of their intervals. Similarly, construct an upstairs graph \(\Lambda_{\Sigma}\).

Every non-boundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, one on each side, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Since the quotient cellulation is finite, \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite graph all of whose vertices have valence \(2\). Therefore every component is a circle.

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles corresponding to edges of \(C\). Across a non-boundary edge, adjacent triangles belong to the same component of \(\Lambda_M\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share non-boundary edges; hence all triangles incident to that boundary vertex also belong to the same component. It follows that \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same open-closed argument upstairs shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is therefore a connected covering of a circle. It is not a finite circle covering. Indeed, no nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) fixes a lifted leaf. If a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x,0)\) and \((x+t,1)\), then an even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) fixes it only if \(2n\beta=0\), and an odd power would have to interchange its endpoints, forcing simultaneously \(t=(2n+1)\beta\) and \(t=-(2n+1)\beta\), impossible because \(\beta>0\). Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient each lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate straight segment, so it has an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line varies continuously with the leaf parameter. Across a shared non-boundary edge the two limiting image segments agree, so the support line is well-defined on the glued leaf graph.

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment is the same geometric segment with the opposite orientation. Therefore, along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\), the path of oriented support lines starts at some oriented line and ends at the same line with the opposite orientation.

#### 4. Oriented-line lemma

An oriented line in \(\mathbb R^3\) can be represented by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) and extend them by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)
\]
satisfies
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanished. Then normalizing would give a continuous map
\[
\phi:S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1.
\]
On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. But on the middle circle \(\delta=\tfrac12\),
\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12).
\]
A map \(\psi:S^1\to S^1\) satisfying \(\psi(s+\tfrac12)=-\psi(s)\) has odd degree: lifting \(\psi\) to an angle function shows that the angle increases over a half-period by an odd multiple of \(\pi\), and hence over a full period by an odd multiple of \(2\pi\). This contradicts the fact that \(\psi\) is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map on \(\delta=0\). Therefore \((a,b)\) vanishes somewhere.

At such a point, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two oriented lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the equation \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar. Hence they intersect. Thus any path of oriented lines ending at the opposite of its start contains two perpendicular intersecting unoriented support lines.

Applying this to the support-line path from the leaf graph gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at some point \(P\in\mathbb R^3\).

#### 5. Leaf displacement and boundary arcs

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Its flat length is
\[
|J|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

We first prove
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]
If \(t=\beta\), then the two endpoints are identified by \(G_\beta\), so their images under \(f\) coincide, contradicting the positive image length of \(J\). The same argument using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) excludes \(t=-\beta\).

If \(t>\beta\), translate \(x\) to \(0\). The leaf \(J\) is the segment
\[
(0,0)\to(t,1),
\]
while \(G_\beta J\), oriented from bottom to top, is the segment
\[
(t+\beta,0)\to(\beta,1).
\]
At height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1),
\]
these two segments meet. Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the image of the nondegenerate segment \(J\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is identical, using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Hence \(|t|<\beta\).

Parametrize the quotient boundary by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\). A bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\), while a top point \((v,1)\) corresponds to bottom coordinate \(v-\beta\). Therefore the endpoints of a leaf with endpoints \((x,0)\) and \((x+t,1)\) cut the boundary circle into two arcs of flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]
Replacing the lift by its \(G_\beta\)-translate changes \(t\) to \(-t\), so for either chosen boundary arc we may choose the lift so that that arc has length \(\beta+t\).

#### 6. The metric conclusion

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the two quotient leaves whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). The two image segments themselves need not both contain \(P\). We now handle all cases.

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), though they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. Therefore at least one of the two image segments lies in a closed ray from \(P\) on its support line. Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By the squeeze-cevian lemma,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Thus, writing
\[
y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T)),
\]
we have
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because then \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), forcing \(y=0\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1),
\]
and choose the lift so that the boundary arc \(D\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Then
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Let
\[
L=|F(T)|.
\]
By the squeeze-cevian lemma,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), boundary contraction gives
\[
\ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Since \(F(H)\) connects the endpoints of \(F(T)\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects the endpoints \(A,C\) of \(F(T)\) and passes through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(|A-C|=L\) and \(Q\) is at distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting \(Q\) orthogonally to the line \(AC\), the left side is minimized when the projection is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary contraction gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
and therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

### Part II. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

We now construct realizations for all \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

#### 7. Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction

We use the following explicit classical construction.

**Lemma 7.1, Sadowsky three-cylinder band.**  
For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there exists an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), developable, isometric Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), with a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. Equivalently, its lift is a map
\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
satisfying
\[
E_\mu(X+\mu,1-Y)=E_\mu(X,Y),
\]
which is quotient-injective, isometric on the flat band, and maps each full-width ruling segment affinely and isometrically to a straight segment in \(\mathbb R^3\).

**Construction and width estimate.**  
Sadowsky’s construction threads a rectangular strip over three circular rods of diameters \(d,d,2d\). The surface consists of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces, so it is developable. The planar pieces meet the cylindrical pieces along common tangent generators, so the tangent plane is continuous; hence the surface is \(C^1\) and piecewise smooth. In the standard notation, the projected midline of the planar parts lies on an equilateral triangle of side length \(L\), the total midline length is \(l=3L\), and the shortest straight midline portions have length
\[
BC=AF=L-\frac{\sqrt3\pi d}{2}.
\]
The full-width generators meet the midline at angle \(60^\circ\), and the non-overlap condition gives
\[
b\le \frac{BC}{2}\tan 60^\circ
= \frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4},
\]
where \(2b\) is the strip width. These are the elementary computations in Sadowsky’s construction. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

Now set \(l=\mu\) and \(b=1/2\). Since \(\mu>\sqrt3\), choose
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Thus the strict width inequality holds for a width-one band. The strict inequality gives positive clearance, so the constructed developable band is embedded. The cylinder generators and the corresponding straight generator lines in the planar pieces give the required continuous full-width ruling foliation. This proves the lemma.

#### 8. A PL secant approximation lemma

For \(L,c>0\), write
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]
on \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\).

**Lemma 8.1, clean PL secant approximation.**  
Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be the lift of a compact \(C^1\) embedded isometric ruled Möbius band of width \(c\) and length \(L\), invariant under \(H_{L,c}\), quotient-injective, and equipped with a continuous full-width ruling foliation. Parametrize the lifted rulings by their lower endpoints:
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)],
\]
where \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous and strictly increasing. Let
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
Then \(H_{L,c}(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)}\). Fix a fundamental interval \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\), and put
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there is an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective.
2. Boundary edges are nonexpanded.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.**  
Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]
Choose a partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so fine that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]
for all \(i\). Extend it equivariantly by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Let
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The consecutive rulings
\[
R_i=[B_i,T_i],\qquad R_{i+1}=[B_{i+1},T_{i+1}]
\]
bound a clean quadrilateral
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}(B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i).
\]
Because \(h\) is strictly increasing, these quadrilaterals tile the strip.

Triangulate each \(Q_i\) by a diagonal, choosing the diagonals \(B_iT_{i+1}\) for \(i=0,\dots,N-1\), and then extending by \(H_{L,c}\)-invariance. Thus the \(H_{L,c}\)-image quadrilateral receives the opposite diagonal. Extending by \(H_{L,c}^2\) gives an invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over each triangle.

Boundary edges are mapped to chords of the smooth boundary curves of the isometric band, so their image lengths are no larger than the corresponding flat boundary lengths.

For transverse edges, ruling edges are exact:
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]
Now consider a forward diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}},\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]
Since \(T_i\) and \(T_{i+1}\) are within flat distance \(<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also, since \(E\) is isometric on the boundary,
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r-\rho\\
&\ge d-2\rho\\
&>d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The backward diagonal estimate is identical, using the lower boundary increment.

Finally, the horizontal span of a ruling edge is at most \(S\), and the horizontal span of a diagonal is at most \(S+\rho<D\).

It remains to justify quotient-injectivity. For sufficiently fine partitions, the secant PL surface is \(C^0\)-close to the \(C^1\) embedded surface \(E\), and its facet tangent planes are uniformly close to the tangent planes of \(E\). This is the standard compact \(C^1\) secant approximation fact: include the finitely many seams among the chosen rulings, and use uniform continuity of the tangent plane on each smooth piece together with the \(C^1\) matching across seams. A compact \(C^1\) embedded surface has a tubular neighborhood with nearest-point projection. After refining the partition, the composition of this projection with the quotient PL map is a local homeomorphism homotopic to the identity on the compact Möbius band. Hence it is a degree-one covering, therefore a homeomorphism. Thus the quotient PL map is embedded, and its lift is quotient-injective. ∎

#### 9. Pulling the secant band back to the required strip

Fix
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
Let \(E_\mu\) be the width-one, length-\(\mu\) Sadowsky band from Lemma 7.1. Let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound over one fundamental interval, and choose
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]
Set
\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu},\qquad L=a\beta=\mu c.
\]
Then \(c>1\).

Scale the Sadowsky band by the factor \(c\). We obtain a width-\(c\), length-\(L\), ruled embedded band on \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\), invariant under
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Its horizontal ruling-span bound is \(cS_\mu\), so \(cD_\mu\) is an admissible span bound.

We need a positive margin for later length estimates. For \(0\le u\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Psi(a,u)
=
\sqrt{c^2(u^2+1)}
-
\sqrt{\frac{c^2u^2}{a^2}+1},
\qquad c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}.
\]
At \(a=1\), we have \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and therefore
\[
\Psi(1,u)
=
\sqrt{c^2(u^2+1)}
-
\sqrt{c^2u^2+1}
>0
\]
for all \(0\le u\le D_\mu\). By compactness and continuity, for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),
\[
m:=\min_{0\le u\le D_\mu}\Psi(a,u)>0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\min_{0\le t\le cD_\mu}
\left(
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
\right)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]

Apply Lemma 8.1 to the scaled Sadowsky band with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]
We obtain an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\).

Define the affine map
\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Because \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
A(x+\beta,1-y)
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Set
\[
f=F_0\circ A,
\]
and let
\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0).
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on each triangle.

We check the squeeze inequalities. Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A\) multiplies its flat length by \(a\), and \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges. Hence
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|,
\]
because \(a<1\). Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a transverse edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(t\) be the horizontal span of the target edge \(A(p)A(q)\). Then \(0\le t<cD_\mu\). Its flat length in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) is
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{t^2+c^2}.
\]
Its original flat length in \(\Sigma\) is
\[
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}.
\]
By Lemma 8.1 and the choice of \(\varepsilon\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\ge
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon
>
\sqrt{t^2/a^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]
Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally, the quotient-injectivity condition holds exactly. For \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\iff Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Therefore every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining this upper construction with the lower bound from Part I, the set of realized numbers is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
In particular, its infimum is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]
This proves the theorem.

## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers, let \(\mathbb R\) be the real numbers, let
\[
\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1],
\qquad
\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\},
\]
and, for \(\beta>0\), let
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).
\]
For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the usual \(k\)-fold iterate, using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) for negative \(k\), and the identity for \(k=0\). A clean triangle is the convex hull in \(\Sigma\) of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). A clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) is a countable locally finite family of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors whose union is \(\Sigma\). A clean triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) which strictly shortens the unique edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\) and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there are a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) such that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\), and
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z
\]
for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\).

We prove exactly that the set of realized \(\beta\)’s is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\). In particular, its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This is not a relaxation of the stated problem.

## Proof

We write \(|\cdot|\) for Euclidean length in the flat strip or in \(\mathbb R^3\), according to context. The proof has two parts.

First, we prove the lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Second, we prove the upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

# Part I. The lower bound

Assume throughout Part I that \(\beta>0\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

## 1. Cevian lengthening and face-to-face refinement

Let \(\Delta\) be a clean triangle. Since its vertices lie on the two boundary components of \(\Sigma\), exactly two of its vertices lie on one boundary component and form the unique boundary edge. Write
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\},
\]
where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\in\partial\Sigma\) is the opposite vertex.

Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(0\le s\le1\), set
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1
\]
and define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
Because \(A\) strictly lengthens the two nonboundary edges,
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Because \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\left(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\right).
\]
The boundary edge is strictly shortened, so \(q''(s)<0\). Hence \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0
\]
for every \(s\in[0,1]\). Thus every cevian from \(a\) to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened:
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|.
\]

We now make the triangulation face-to-face. In a clean triangulation, nonboundary edges are already face-to-face: two nonboundary clean edges either are disjoint, meet only in boundary endpoints, or coincide. Indeed, if two full-width nonboundary edges overlap in a nontrivial segment, they lie on the same line and hence coincide; if they crossed transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\), the local planar tiling near the crossing would force interiors of triangles to overlap. The only possible non-face-to-face phenomenon is that boundary vertices subdivide boundary edges.

For each triangle \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), list all boundary vertices of the triangulation lying on the boundary segment \([r_0,r_1]\):
\[
r_0=s_0,s_1,\dots,s_m=r_1
\]
in order. Local finiteness implies this list is finite. Replace \(\Delta\) by the clean triangles
\[
\operatorname{conv}\{a,s_i,s_{i+1}\},\qquad i=0,\dots,m-1.
\]
Doing this equivariantly for all triangles gives a \(G_\beta\)-invariant face-to-face clean refinement. The same map \(f\) is still affine on each refined triangle. The new boundary edges are subsegments of old boundary edges, hence are strictly shortened. The new nonboundary edges are cevians of old triangles, hence are strictly lengthened by the cevian lemma. Thus, after this harmless refinement, we may assume from now on that \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face.

Two immediate consequences will be used repeatedly:

1. every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in image path length;
2. every leaf segment, meaning every cevian in a triangle, has nonzero image length and is strictly lengthened.

---

## 2. The quotient is a finite PL embedded Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is a single circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The orbit condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\). Therefore \(f\) descends to a map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The same orbit condition says exactly that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We next record that the quotient cellulation is finite and PL. For \(k\ne0\), the displacement of \(G_\beta^k\) is at least \(\beta\): even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while odd powers change \(x\) by an odd multiple of \(\beta\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were non-injective on a triangle \(\Delta\), then, because \(f|_\Delta\) is affine, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f|_\Delta\) is injective on every triangle.

Therefore no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting the fact that \(G_\beta^k\) has no fixed point for \(k\ne0\).

A compact fundamental domain meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness. Thus there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of triangles, and the quotient is a finite regular PL cellulation of the Möbius band. The descended map \(F\) is affine and injective on each quotient triangle.

---

## 3. The clean leaf space is one circle

For each clean triangle \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), consider the interval of cevians
\[
[a,r(s)],\qquad 0\le s\le1.
\]
The two endpoints \(s=0,1\) correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\Delta\). Glue two such endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary edges are identified in the face-to-face quotient triangulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), called the leaf graph.

Each nonboundary edge of a surface triangulation is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence every connected component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We show \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all quotient triangles corresponding to edges of \(C\). If a triangle in \(U_C\) meets another triangle across a nonboundary edge, then the corresponding intervals in \(\Lambda_M\) share an endpoint, so the adjacent triangle is also in \(U_C\).

At a boundary vertex \(v\), the incident triangles form a fan
\[
T_1,\dots,T_m.
\]
Consecutive triangles in this fan share nonboundary edges, except for the two extreme boundary edges. Thus all triangles incident to \(v\) lie in the same component of \(\Lambda_M\). It follows that \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is connected:
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same open-closed argument upstairs in \(\Sigma\) shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The covering map
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a connected covering of a circle. It cannot be a finite circle cover, because no nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
An even nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), and an odd power swaps the boundary components; equality of the same unordered segment would force simultaneously \(t=(2m+1)\beta\) and \(t=-(2m+1)\beta\), impossible. Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image is a nondegenerate straight segment, so it has an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line varies continuously with the leaf parameter. Inside one triangle this is immediate from affine dependence on the cevian parameter; at a shared nonboundary edge the two limiting segments agree with the same bottom-to-top orientation.

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\), oriented from bottom to top, has endpoints \(G_\beta t\) and \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the geometric image segment is the same but with opposite orientation. Therefore, as one moves along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\), the path of oriented support lines satisfies
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

---

## 4. Oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma

We need the following elementary lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]
Then two distinct lines in the family are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. The vector \(m\) is independent of the choice of \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all real \(s\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Let
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta).
\]
Using the antiperiodicity above,
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanished. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
would define a continuous map from the cylinder \(S^1\times[0,1]\) to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map with this symmetry has odd degree: lifting its argument \(\theta(s)\), one has
\[
\theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi n
\]
for some integer \(n\), and therefore the total degree is \(1+2n\). But the middle circle is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant bottom circle, so its degree must be \(0\), a contradiction. Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere. This zero cannot occur at \(\delta=0\) or \(\delta=1\).

Now take the two lines corresponding to \(s\) and \(s+\delta\). The equation \(a=0\) says their directions \(u,v\) are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by
\[
(u,p\times u),\qquad (v,q\times v),
\]
then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
For nonparallel lines, the vanishing of this scalar triple product is exactly the condition that the two lines are coplanar. Since here the directions are perpendicular, the two lines are nonparallel; coplanar nonparallel lines intersect. Thus the family contains two distinct perpendicular intersecting lines. \(\square\)

Applying this lemma to the oriented support-line path obtained from the leaf circle, we get two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

---

## 5. Leaf displacement and boundary arc lengths

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf, oriented from bottom to top, with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Its flat length is
\[
|J|_0=\sqrt{1+s^2}.
\]

We claim
\[
|s|<\beta.
\]
If \(s=\beta\), then the top endpoint is \(G_\beta(x,0)\), so the two endpoints have the same image under \(f\), contradicting the fact that the image of \(J\) is a nondegenerate segment. The case \(s=-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\).

If \(s>\beta\), translate horizontally so the leaf is the segment from \((0,0)\) to \((s,1)\). Its \(G_\beta\)-translate is the segment from
\[
(s+\beta,0)
\quad\text{to}\quad
(\beta,1).
\]
These two segments meet in their relative interiors at height
\[
y=\frac{s+\beta}{2s}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the segment \(J\). The case \(s<-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Therefore \(|s|<\beta\).

The quotient boundary is a circle of length \(2\beta\). Parametrize it by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\). The top point \((x+s,1)\) is equivalent to the bottom point with coordinate \(x+s-\beta\) modulo \(2\beta\). Since \(|s|<\beta\), the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of the quotient leaf have flat lengths
\[
\beta-s,\qquad \beta+s.
\]

For the metric argument below, it is best to define the parameter \(t\) through boundary arc lengths, not by choosing a preferred lift. Given a quotient leaf \(T\) and one of the two boundary arcs \(D\) between its endpoints, define \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\) by
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\qquad
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(H\) is the other boundary arc and \(\ell_0\) denotes flat boundary length. For some lift of \(T\), \(t\) is equal to the horizontal displacement \(s\) or \(-s\). Hence the flat length of \(T\) is
\[
|T|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

---

## 6. Metric endgame

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the two distinct quotient leaves whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Write \(F(T)\) and \(F(B)\) for the image segments.

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the Möbius band; they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Therefore at least one of the two image segments is contained in a closed ray from \(P\) along its support line. Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The cevian lemma gives
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Thus
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because if it were, then \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), giving \(y=0\). This handles the possible shared-endpoint cases: the other endpoint of \(B\) may coincide with an endpoint of \(T\), but \(q\) does not.

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc between the endpoints of \(T\) that contains \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Define \(t\) by
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\qquad
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
By the cevian lemma,
\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

### The \(H\)-inequality

Boundary contraction gives
\[
\ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(\ell_F\) denotes image path length. Since \(F(H)\) is a path from \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\boxed{\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t.}
\]

### The \(D\)-inequality

The path \(F(D)\) goes from \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(|A-C|=L\) and \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting \(Q\) orthogonally to the line \(AC\), the left side is minimized when the projection lies at the midpoint of \(AC\).

Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary contraction gives
\[
\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\boxed{\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t.}
\]

Combining the two boxed inequalities,
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Therefore every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

# Part II. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

The construction uses a classical explicit developable Möbius band due to Sadowsky. We state the precise form needed, with the relevant normalization.

## 7. Sadowsky three-cylinder lemma

**Sadowsky three-cylinder lemma.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there exists an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric Möbius band
\[
I_\mu:M_{\mu,1}\to\mathbb R^3,
\]
where
\[
M_{\mu,1}=([0,\mu]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\mu,1-y),
\]
with the following properties:

1. the induced intrinsic metric is the flat metric of the rectangle;
2. the image is embedded with positive clearance;
3. the surface is made from finitely many planar pieces and cylindrical pieces meeting \(C^1\)-smoothly along rulings;
4. it has a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings, meaning each ruling joins one boundary component to the other and maps to a straight segment in \(\mathbb R^3\).

The construction is Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction. In its standard notation, for total length \(l\), half-width \(b\), and cylinder diameter parameter \(d>0\), the construction works whenever
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
It consists of three planar parts and three semicylindrical parts; the cylindrical generators and the planar ruling lines form a continuous full-width ruling foliation. Because planes and cylinders are developable and are glued tangent-to-tangent along rulings, the resulting surface is \(C^1\) and path-isometric to the original rectangle. The strict inequality above gives positive separation between the distinct pieces, hence positive clearance.

For our normalization, take \(l=\mu\) and \(b=1/2\). Since \(\mu>\sqrt3\), choose
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Thus the construction gives a width-one, length-\(\mu\), embedded path-isometric Möbius band with the stated ruling foliation.

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives the same statement for width \(c\) and length
\[
L=\mu c.
\]
Let
\[
M_{L,c}=([0,L]\times[0,c])/(0,Y)\sim(L,c-Y).
\]
The lift to the infinite strip \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) will be denoted
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
It is invariant under
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]
and it is quotient-injective:
\[
E(P)=E(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

The lifted rulings can be written uniquely as
\[
R_x=[B_x,T_x],
\qquad
B_x=(x,0),\qquad
T_x=(h(x),c),
\]
where \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is a continuous strictly increasing homeomorphism. The deck map sends \(R_x\) to \(R_{\phi(x)}\), where
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L,
\]
and the top endpoint relation gives
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
The quantity \(|h(x)-x|\) is bounded globally; indeed, on a fundamental leaf interval \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\), set
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
The relation \(h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x)\) extends this bound to all \(x\).

---

## 8. Invariant clean secant-PL approximation

We now prove the PL approximation lemma needed for the construction.

**Lemma.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be a lifted Sadowsky band of width \(c\), length \(L\), and deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Let \(S\) be the global bound for the horizontal spans of the rulings:
\[
|h(x)-x|\le S.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z;
   \]
2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|_{\mathrm{flat}}-\varepsilon;
   \]
4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,
\qquad
2\rho<\varepsilon,
\]
and small enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Pick \(x_0\). Since \(\phi(x_0)>x_0\), choose a finite partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so fine that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,
\qquad
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]
for every \(i\). Include among the \(x_i\)’s all seam rulings of the finitely many planar and cylindrical pieces in one quotient period.

Extend the sequence by the deck rule
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]
Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),
\qquad
T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The rulings
\[
R_i=[B_i,T_i]
\]
cut the strip into clean quadrilaterals
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]

Now triangulate anti-periodically under the half-twist. For \(i=0,\dots,N-1\), triangulate \(Q_i\) by the diagonal
\[
B_iT_{i+1}.
\]
Since \(H\) swaps the two boundary components, it sends this diagonal to the opposite diagonal in \(Q_{i+N}\):
\[
H(B_iT_{i+1})=T_{i+N}B_{i+N+1}.
\]
Therefore triangulate \(Q_{i+N}\) by the diagonal
\[
T_{i+N}B_{i+N+1}.
\]
Extend by \(H^2\). This gives a genuinely \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),
\qquad
F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. The vertex assignment and the anti-periodic diagonal choice imply
\[
F_0\circ H=F_0.
\]

We next prove embeddedness on the quotient. On the compact quotient Möbius band, the smooth Sadowsky surface is \(C^1\) embedded. In ruling coordinates, each smooth piece has the form
\[
G(u,s)=(1-s)\gamma_0(u)+s\gamma_1(u),
\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
where the curves \(\gamma_0,\gamma_1\) are \(C^1\) on each planar or cylindrical piece and match \(C^1\) across seam rulings. Because the seam rulings were included in the partition, every secant strip lies inside one smooth piece. As the maximum of the bottom and top endpoint increments tends to \(0\), the two affine triangles spanning a strip converge uniformly and in tangent plane to the corresponding ruled surface strip.

A compact \(C^1\) embedded surface has local graph neighborhoods and a tubular neighborhood in the following elementary sense: finitely many coordinate neighborhoods cover the quotient, in each of which the surface is a \(C^1\) graph over a plane; outside a sufficiently small neighborhood of the diagonal in the domain, distinct image points are separated by a positive distance. For sufficiently small \(\rho\), every secant triangle lies in one of these graph neighborhoods and is itself a graph over the same plane with small slope, while the whole PL surface is uniformly close to the smooth embedded surface. Therefore no local folding and no nonlocal self-intersection can occur. Thus the induced quotient PL map is embedded.

Equivalently, the nearest local graph projection from the PL surface to the smooth surface gives a map of the quotient Möbius band uniformly close to the identity and locally injective; by compactness and the positive separation of non-near points, it is globally injective. Lifting back to the infinite strip gives quotient-injectivity:
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

Now we prove the edge estimates.

Boundary edges are chords of the smooth boundary curves. Since \(E\) is path-isometric, each such chord has length at most the corresponding flat boundary arc. Hence boundary edges are nonexpanded.

Ruling edges are exact: if the edge is \(B_iT_i\), then \(E(B_i)E(T_i)\) is a straight image of a flat ruling, so
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]

Consider a forward diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Put
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}},
\qquad
r=|B_i-T_i|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]
Since \(T_i\) and \(T_{i+1}\) differ horizontally by \(<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le |T_i-T_{i+1}|_{\mathrm{flat}}<\rho.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r-\rho\\
&\ge d-2\rho\\
&>d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The opposite diagonal \(T_iB_{i+1}\) is estimated identically, using the bottom increment \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\).

Finally, every ruling has horizontal span at most \(S\), and every diagonal has horizontal span \(<S+\rho<D\). This proves the lemma. \(\square\)

---

## 9. Anisotropic pullback

Let \(\beta>\sqrt3\) be given. Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
Take the width-one Sadowsky band of length \(\mu\), and let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound. Choose a number
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

Choose \(a<1\), close enough to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]
Define
\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}.
\]
Then \(c>1\), and if
\[
L=\mu c,
\]
then
\[
L=a\beta.
\]

Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\). It now has width \(c\), length \(L=\mu c\), and ruling-span bound \(cS_\mu\). Apply the secant-PL lemma with transverse horizontal-span bound
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]

We must choose \(a\) and the secant error \(\varepsilon\) so that transverse edges become strictly lengthened after pullback. For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)
=
\sqrt{c^2s^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{\frac{c^2s^2}{a^2}+1},
\qquad
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}.
\]
At \(a=1\), one has \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and
\[
\sqrt{c^2s^2+c^2}
>
\sqrt{c^2s^2+1}
\]
for every \(s\). Hence \(\Delta_1(s)>0\) on the compact interval \([0,D_\mu]\). By continuity, for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\), still
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]
Apply the secant-PL lemma with this \(\varepsilon\).

Now define the anisotropic affine map
\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Then
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
(ax+L,c-cy)
=
H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Let
\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

We verify the squeeze inequalities.

### Boundary edges

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Its image \(A(p)A(q)\) has flat length
\[
a|p-q|.
\]
Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### Nonboundary edges

Let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(t\) be the horizontal span of the edge \(A(p)A(q)\). By construction,
\[
0\le t\le cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), with \(0\le s\le D_\mu\). In the scaled Sadowsky strip,
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}.
\]
In the original strip \(\Sigma\),
\[
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}
=
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}.
\]
The secant estimate gives
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon.
\]
Because \(\varepsilon<m\le\Delta_a(s)\),
\[
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon
>
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}
=
|p-q|_{\mathrm{flat}}.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

### Deck-orbit injectivity

Finally, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\circ G_\beta=H_{L,c}\circ A\) and \(A\) is injective.

Hence \(f\) realizes \(\beta\).

Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\) was arbitrary, every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

Combining Part I and Part II, the realized numbers are exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Therefore the infimum of the set of realized numbers is
\[
\boxed{\sqrt3}.
\]

## Claim

With the definitions in the problem, the realized numbers are exactly  
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
In particular, the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

---

# Proof

Write \(G=G_\beta\). We prove first that every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), and then construct realizations for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

## 1. The lower bound

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\).

### 1.1. The triangulation is face-to-face

A clean triangle has exactly one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it its boundary edge. The other two edges connect the two boundary components and will be called transverse edges.

We need the following elementary consequence of convexity, local finiteness, and disjoint interiors.

**Lemma 1.** The clean triangulation is face-to-face: distinct edges have disjoint relative interiors, every transverse edge is incident to exactly two triangles, and every boundary edge is incident to exactly one triangle.

**Proof.** Two transverse edges cannot cross transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\). Indeed, near a transverse crossing, each of the two incident triangles occupies an open half-neighborhood on one side of its supporting line. Two transverse lines have half-planes whose interiors meet in an open sector, forcing two triangle interiors to overlap.

If two transverse edges are collinear and share more than one point, they are the same edge, because a nonhorizontal line meets the two boundary lines \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in exactly two points, so the segment inside \(\Sigma\) is uniquely determined.

Boundary edges cannot overlap in a nontrivial interval: at an interior point of such an overlap, both incident triangles contain a small inward half-disk, again contradicting disjoint interiors. Similarly, no endpoint of a transverse edge can lie in the relative interior of a boundary edge, because a triangle having that boundary edge contains a small inward half-disk near the point, while the triangle having the transverse edge contains a nonempty sector entering that half-disk.

Thus intersections of triangles are unions of common faces. Now let \(e\) be a transverse edge and let \(z\) be an interior point of \(e\). A small disk around \(z\) is divided by \(e\) into two sides. Disjointness of interiors gives at most one incident triangle on each side, while the union condition forces at least one on each side. Hence exactly two triangles meet along \(e\). The same local argument at an interior point of a boundary edge gives exactly one incident triangle. ∎

### 1.2. Passage to the quotient

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
M_\beta=[0,\beta]\times[0,1]/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y),
\]
so \(M_\beta\) is a compact Möbius band. Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The realization condition implies \(f\circ G=f\), so \(f\) descends to a map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), hence \(p=G^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), so \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need that the quotient triangulation is finite. First observe that \(f\) is injective on every triangle. Indeed, if \(f|_\Delta\) had affine rank \(<2\), then there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would give \(p=G^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\). But every nontrivial \(G^k\) moves every point by Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\), a contradiction for \(p,q\) sufficiently close.

Consequently no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G^k\Delta\), then \(z=G^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Since \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the \(G\)-action unless \(k=0\).

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, and every \(G\)-orbit of triangles has a representative meeting such a rectangle. Hence the quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\) has finitely many triangles.

### 1.3. A cevian lengthening lemma

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(0\le s\le1\), put
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1.
\]
Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two transverse edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Hence every segment from the apex of a clean triangle to a point on its boundary edge is strictly lengthened by \(f\).

In particular, every such segment has nonzero image length.

### 1.4. The leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider the one-parameter family of segments joining the apex to the opposite boundary edge. These will be called leaves. The two endpoint leaves of this family are precisely the two transverse edges of the triangle.

For every quotient triangle of \(M_\beta\), take one interval parametrizing its leaves. Whenever two triangles share a transverse edge, glue the corresponding endpoints of their intervals. By Lemma 1, every transverse edge has valence \(2\), so the resulting finite graph \(\Lambda_M\) has every vertex of valence \(2\).

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles corresponding to edges of \(C\). Across a transverse edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same component. Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share transverse edges, so they also belong to the same component. Hence \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. A connected finite graph with every vertex of valence \(2\) is a circle, so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same construction upstairs gives a graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). The same open-closed argument shows \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. Moreover \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is a connected covering. It cannot be a finite circle cover: if some nonzero power \(G^k\) fixed a lifted leaf with endpoints \((x,0)\) and \((x+t,1)\), then for even \(k=2n\) we would need \(2n\beta=0\), while for odd \(k=2n+1\) we would need simultaneously
\[
t=(2n+1)\beta,\qquad t=-(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Thus
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate straight segment, hence determines an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line varies continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

If \(\ell\) is a lifted leaf, then \(G\ell\), when oriented from bottom to top, has the same image segment with the opposite orientation, because \(f\circ G=f\) and \(G\) exchanges the two boundary components. Thus along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G\ell\), the oriented support line travels from some oriented line to the same unoriented line with opposite orientation.

### 1.5. A lemma on oriented lines

An oriented line in \(\mathbb R^3\) can be represented by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

**Lemma 2.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist two distinct parameters \(s_1,s_2\) for which the corresponding unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(L_s\) and extend them to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, with
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Normalizing gives a map
\[
\Phi:S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1.
\]
On \(\delta=0\), \(\Phi\) is constant. But on \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\Phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\Phi(s,\tfrac12).
\]
A map \(\psi:S^1\to S^1\) satisfying \(\psi(s+\frac12)=-\psi(s)\) has odd degree: lifting to an angle function, the angle changes over a half-period by an odd multiple of \(\pi\), hence over a full period by an odd multiple of \(2\pi\). This contradicts the fact that \(\psi\) is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map at \(\delta=0\). Therefore \((a,b)\) vanishes somewhere.

At a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two oriented lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the condition \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. ∎

Applying Lemma 2 to the support-line path coming from \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), we obtain two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at some point \(P\in\mathbb R^3\).

### 1.6. Geometry of lifted leaves

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Its flat length is
\[
|J|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

We first prove
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]
If \(t=\beta\), then the two endpoints are identified by \(G\), so their images coincide, contradicting the fact that \(f(J)\) has positive length. Similarly \(t=-\beta\) is impossible.

If \(t>\beta\), translate horizontally and assume the leaf is
\[
(0,0)\to(t,1).
\]
Then \(GJ\), oriented from bottom to top, is
\[
(t+\beta,0)\to(\beta,1).
\]
These two segments intersect at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G w\), hence \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate segment \(J\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is identical using \(G^{-1}\). Hence \(|t|<\beta\).

Now parametrize the quotient boundary by a coordinate modulo \(2\beta\): a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\), while a top point \((v,1)\) has coordinate \(v-\beta\). Thus the endpoints of a leaf
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1)
\]
cut the boundary circle into arcs of flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]

### 1.7. The metric inequality

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the two quotient leaves whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\).

The relative interiors of distinct leaves in \(M_\beta\) are disjoint. Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. Hence at least one of the two image segments lies entirely in a closed ray from \(P\) on its support line. Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By the cevian lengthening lemma,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Therefore, writing
\[
y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T)),
\]
we have
\[
y>1.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
If necessary, replace this lift by its \(G\)-translate; this changes \(t\) to \(-t\). We choose the lift so that the boundary arc \(D\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc, so
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Let
\[
L=|F(T)|.
\]
By the cevian lengthening lemma,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

The image of every nontrivial boundary arc is strictly shorter than its flat length, because each boundary edge is strictly shortened and the quotient triangulation is finite. Since \(F(H)\) connects the two endpoints of \(F(T)\),
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

Now \(F(D)\) connects the two endpoints of \(F(T)\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|,
\]
where \(A,C\) are the endpoints of \(F(T)\). Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\) and \(|A-C|=L\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary contraction gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Thus every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

---

## 2. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

### 2.1. A ruled embedded Möbius band of length \(>\sqrt3\)

We use the following explicit form of Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction.

**Lemma 3.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there exists an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), developable, isometric Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), equipped with a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. In lifted form, there is a map
\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that
\[
E_\mu(X+\mu,1-Y)=E_\mu(X,Y),
\]
the quotient map is embedded, each full-width ruling maps affinely and isometrically to a straight segment, and the boundary curves are parametrized by arclength. Moreover, the construction extends to a slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) ruled band.

**Proof.** Sadowsky’s construction threads a rectangular strip over three circular rods of diameters \(d,d,2d\). The surface consists cyclically of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces. Each piece is isometric to its flat development, and adjacent pieces meet along a common generator with matching tangent planes. Hence the resulting surface is \(C^1\), piecewise smooth, developable, and isometric.

Let the strip length be \(\mu\), and let \(2b\) be its width. In the standard symmetric three-cylinder geometry, the projected midline of the planar parts lies on an equilateral triangle of side length \(\mu/3\), and the full-width generators meet the midline at angle \(60^\circ\). The non-overlap computation gives the maximal half-width
\[
b_{\max}=\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Choose
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]
Then
\[
b_{\max}>\frac12.
\]
Thus a strip of half-width \(b=\frac12\), i.e. width \(1\), lies strictly below the non-overlap threshold.

Because the inequality is strict, the same construction works for a slightly larger half-width \(b=\frac12+\eta\). The resulting wider band is compactly embedded; restricting to the width-one subband gives positive clearance from all nonadjacent pieces. The cylinder generators and their straight continuations in the planar pieces give a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. Passing to the infinite lift gives the stated map \(E_\mu\) with
\[
E_\mu(X+\mu,1-Y)=E_\mu(X,Y).
\]
Embeddedness of the quotient gives quotient-injectivity. ∎

### 2.2. A PL secant approximation lemma

Let \(c,L>0\), and define
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]
on \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\).

We need a PL approximation of a ruled embedded band that remains embedded on the quotient.

**Lemma 4.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be the lift of an embedded compact ruled Möbius band invariant under \(H_{L,c}\), with a slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) ruled extension. Assume the band is foliated by full-width straight rulings
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)],
\]
and that \(E\) maps every ruling affinely and isometrically to a straight segment, while the boundary curves are parametrized by arclength.

Fix a fundamental interval \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\), where
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
Let
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\) iff \(P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).
2. Boundary edges are nonexpanded.
3. Every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Because the rulings form a continuous nonintersecting foliation, \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is a continuous strictly increasing homeomorphism. The map \(H_{L,c}\) sends \(R_x\) to the ruling whose lower endpoint is \(h(x)+L\), so
\[
H_{L,c}(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},\qquad \phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
Its upper endpoint relation gives
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Hence
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]

We also have \(|h(x)-x|<L\). If \(h(x)-x=L\), then the endpoints of \(R_x\) are identified by \(H_{L,c}\), contradicting the fact that the ruling has positive image length. If \(h(x)-x>L\), then \(R_x\) intersects \(H_{L,c}(R_x)\) in an interior point, producing two distinct points on the same ruling with the same image under \(E\), again impossible. The case \(h(x)-x\le -L\) is the same using \(H_{L,c}^{-1}\). Therefore \(\phi(x)>x\), so fundamental intervals exist.

Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]
Choose a partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
fine enough that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]
for all \(i\), and also fine enough to contain all smooth-piece seams of the ruled surface. Extend the sequence by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Put
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The noncrossing segments \(R_i=[B_i,T_i]\) and \(R_{i+1}=[B_{i+1},T_{i+1}]\), together with the boundary intervals \([B_i,B_{i+1}]\) and \([T_i,T_{i+1}]\), bound a clean quadrilateral \(Q_i\). These quadrilaterals tile the strip.

Triangulate \(Q_i\) by the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) for \(0\le i<N\), and extend the choice by \(H_{L,c}\)-invariance. Thus \(H_{L,c}\) sends each chosen diagonal to the opposite diagonal in the image quadrilateral. This gives an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. Since \(E\) is \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant and the triangulation is \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant, \(F_0\) is \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant.

Boundary edges map to chords of boundary arcs of the isometric band, so their image lengths are at most their flat lengths.

For transverse edges, first consider a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\). Since \(E\) maps rulings isometrically,
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Now consider a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Because \(|T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le |T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge r-\rho \\
&> d-2\rho \\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The other type of diagonal is handled identically using the lower boundary increment. Thus every transverse edge is shortened by at most \(\varepsilon\).

The horizontal span of a ruling edge is at most \(S\). The horizontal span of a diagonal is at most \(S+\rho<D\). Hence every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

It remains to ensure quotient-injectivity. Because the original ruled band has a slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) extension, the quotient surface has a tubular neighborhood with nearest-point projection. Since the partition contains all smooth seams, on each smooth ruled patch the surface has the form
\[
E(s,v)=\gamma(s)+v\,\xi(s),
\]
with \(\gamma\) and \(\xi\) smooth and \(v\) the ruling coordinate. As the mesh of the partition tends to \(0\), the secant PL maps converge uniformly to \(E\), and their facet tangent planes converge uniformly to the tangent planes of \(E\). Therefore, for sufficiently fine partitions, the quotient PL surface lies in the tubular neighborhood.

Let \(\pi\) be the nearest-point projection to the slightly wider smooth surface, and let \(E^+\) denote the wider embedding. On the quotient, define
\[
P=(E^+)^{-1}\circ\pi\circ F_0.
\]
For sufficiently fine partitions, \(P\) is piecewise \(C^1\)-close to the inclusion of the original quotient band into the slightly wider parameter band.

A compactness argument shows that every sufficiently \(C^1\)-small piecewise \(C^1\) perturbation of an embedding is injective: locally, derivative closeness to the identity gives injectivity in finitely many coordinate charts; globally, points separated by a fixed positive intrinsic distance have positive separation under the original embedding, and uniform closeness preserves that separation. Hence \(P\) is injective.

If \(F_0(p)=F_0(q)\) on the quotient, then \(P(p)=P(q)\), so \(p=q\). Thus the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting back to \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\), we get
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). ∎

### 2.3. Realizing every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Fix
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
Let \(E_\mu\) be the width-one, length-\(\mu\) ruled band from Lemma 3. Let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound over a fundamental interval, and choose
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]
Set
\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu},\qquad L=a\beta.
\]
Then \(c>1\) and \(L=c\mu\).

Scale the Sadowsky band by the factor \(c\). This gives a width-\(c\), length-\(L\) ruled embedded band invariant under
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Its ruling-span bound is \(cS_\mu\), so \(cD_\mu\) is an admissible span bound.

We choose \(a\) close enough to \(1\) so that
\[
m:=\min_{0\le u\le D_\mu}
\left[
c\sqrt{1+u^2}
-
\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1}
\right]>0.
\]
This is possible because when \(a=1\), we have \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and therefore
\[
c\sqrt{1+u^2}>\sqrt{c^2u^2+1}
\]
for all \(u\). By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\), the minimum is positive for all \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\).

Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]
Apply Lemma 4 to the scaled band with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]
We obtain an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with the four properties of Lemma 4.

Now define the affine map
\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=A(x+\beta,1-y)
=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Set
\[
f=F_0\circ A,
\qquad
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0).
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

We verify the squeeze inequalities.

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A\) multiplies its flat length by \(a\), and \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges. Hence
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|,
\]
because \(a<1\). Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a transverse edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let the horizontal span of \(A(p)A(q)\) be \(t\). Then \(0\le t<cD_\mu\), so \(t=cu\) for some \(0\le u<D_\mu\). The flat length of the target edge is
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=\sqrt{t^2+c^2}=c\sqrt{1+u^2}.
\]
The flat length of the original edge is
\[
|p-q|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)^2+1}
=
\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1}.
\]
By Lemma 4 and the choice of \(\varepsilon<m\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
|f(p)-f(q)|
&=|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)| \\
&\ge \sqrt{t^2+c^2}-\varepsilon \\
&> \sqrt{\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)^2+1} \\
&=|p-q|.
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z \\
&\Longleftrightarrow p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).
