At height \(0<z<h\), the surface slice consists of two side segments: the \(p_2p_0\)-side of the homothetic triangle of scale
\[
1-\eta z/h,
\]
and the \(p_1p_2\)-side of the homothetic triangle of scale
\[
1+\eta z/(2h).
\]
These are adjacent sides of concentric homothetic equilateral triangles with different scales, hence are disjoint.

At height \(h<z<2h\), the slice consists of the \(p_0p_1\)-side of scale
\[
1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h,
\]
and the \(p_1p_2\)-side of scale
\[
1+\eta z/(2h).
\]
These scales are unequal for \(h<z<2h\), so the two adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=h\), the middle horizontal triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), while the extra \(p_1p_2\)-side coming from the \(A\)-to-\(C\) panel has scale \(1+\eta/2\), hence lies outside the middle triangle. At \(z=0\) and \(z=2h\), the side panels meet the horizontal triangles exactly along the prescribed boundary sides and vertices.

Thus the only intersections among the nine target triangles are the common faces prescribed by the quotient cellulation. Hence the quotient PL map is an embedding.

## 4. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

For \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda.
\]

For \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge has length
\[
b-2\lambda.
\]
The target endpoints lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le b-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le b-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<b-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

## 5. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Let
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
A lift of \(e_j\) is the segment from \(X_j\) to \(Y_j\), whose horizontal span is
\[
s_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta)-c_j=d_j-\beta,
\]
where
\[
d_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
From the periodic pattern,
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
b+2\lambda, & j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2b-\lambda, & j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(\beta=3b/2\), we get
\[
|s_j|<\beta/3.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length
\[
\sqrt{1+s_j^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_*.
\]

On the target side, \(P_j\) and \(P_{j+5}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+5}|>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_*\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L_*.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Consequently each affine face map is a squeeze map.

## 6. Lift and orbit-injectivity

Define \(f\) on the strip vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). Because \(P_{j+9}=P_j\),
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) maps triangles affinely to triangles, this gives
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient PL map constructed above.

Therefore, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p\) and \(q\) have the same image in the quotient \(\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), equivalently if and only if
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining Part I and Part II, the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). ∎

## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

For every real number \(\beta>0\), let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), let \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\). For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the usual \(k\)-fold iterate, using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) when \(k<0\) and the identity when \(k=0\). A clean triangle means the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\); its unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) is called its boundary edge. A clean triangulation means a countable, locally finite collection of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\). It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle means an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the boundary edge and strictly lengthens the two other edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) which is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies
\[
f(p)=f(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z
\]
for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\).

The proof below establishes the stronger statement
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Consequently the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\). This is a strengthening of the requested infimum statement, not a relaxation.

## Proof

We prove first that every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), and then construct a realization for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

---

# Part I. Lower bound: a realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. Let \(\mathcal T_0\) be a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and let \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) be a realizing map.

## 1. Cevian lengthening and refinement

A clean triangle has exactly two vertices on one boundary line and one vertex on the other. The edge joining the two vertices on the same boundary line is its boundary edge; the opposite vertex is its apex.

### Lemma 1.1 — Cevian lengthening

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the apex. Let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be an affine squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is concave, so
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Therefore \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened. On the boundary edge, affineness gives a constant contraction factor strictly smaller than \(1\), so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened. \(\square\)

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) as follows. For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of \(\mathcal T_0\) lying on that boundary edge, and cone the resulting subintervals to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies that only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge, so each original triangle is subdivided into finitely many clean triangles.

The refinement is still countable, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant. By Lemma 1.1, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle: new boundary edges are shortened, and new cevians are lengthened.

After this refinement, the triangulation is face-to-face. Boundary vertex-in-edge incidences have been removed by construction. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, because two convex triangles whose edges cross transversely would have overlapping interiors near the crossing. Two collinear nonboundary edges joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) have the same endpoints and hence are the same edge. We write \(\mathcal T\) for the refined triangulation.

## 2. Descent to a finite embedded quotient cellulation

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) is a Euclidean isometry, the Euclidean metric on \(\Sigma\) descends to a flat metric on \(M_\beta\). The quotient is the Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is a single circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization hypothesis \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need finiteness of the quotient cellulation. Every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\): indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus if \(k\ne0\), then \(|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on a refined triangle \(\Delta\), then its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), and there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle.

Consequently no closed refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. For if
\[
z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta,\qquad k\ne0,
\]
then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, so there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of refined triangles. Therefore \(\mathcal T\) descends to a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\), and \(F\) is PL and embedded with respect to this cellulation.

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\). Indeed, subdivide the arc at the finitely many boundary vertices it meets; each nontrivial piece lies in a boundary edge of a clean triangle and is strictly shortened.

## 3. The leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider all cevians from the apex to points of the boundary edge. For each quotient triangle \(\tau\), these cevians are parametrized by a closed interval \(I_\tau\). The endpoints of \(I_\tau\) correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\tau\). Whenever two triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the corresponding endpoints of their intervals. The resulting finite graph is the quotient leaf graph, denoted \(\Lambda_M\).

Every nonboundary edge lies in the interior of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangles, one on each side. Hence every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals belong to \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, except for the two extreme boundary edges. Thus all triangles incident to a boundary vertex lie in the same \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, and so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct similarly the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) using the refined triangulation of \(\Sigma\). The same open-closed argument in the connected strip \(\Sigma\) shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The quotient map \(\Sigma\to M_\beta\) induces a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has one endpoint \((x_0,0)\) and one endpoint \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it can fix the leaf only if \(n=0\). An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) would have to interchange the two endpoints, forcing
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible because \(\beta>0\). Therefore the covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, it is a line:
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from its endpoint on \(y=0\) to its endpoint on \(y=1\). By Lemma 1.1, the image of every lifted leaf is a nondegenerate segment, so it has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented support lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(G_\beta t\) and top endpoint \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment of \(G_\beta\ell\) is the same geometric segment as the image of \(\ell\), but with opposite orientation. Thus along one fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), the path of oriented support lines starts at some oriented line and ends at the opposite oriented line.

## 4. An oriented-line lemma

### Lemma 1.2 — Perpendicular intersecting support lines

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct parameters determine unoriented support lines that are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by its unit direction \(u\in S^2\) and its moment
\[
m=p\times u,
\]
where \(p\) is any point on the line. The moment is independent of the chosen point \(p\), and reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all real \(s\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]

For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
and
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, for the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one checks that
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
Therefore the map \(s\mapsto\phi(s,1/2)\) has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the cylinder \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), so it must have degree \(0\), a contradiction.

Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by points \(p,q\) and directions \(u,v\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 1.2 to the oriented support-line path from the previous section gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect. Let those support lines be \(\ell_T\) and \(\ell_B\), and let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap\ell_B.
\]

## 5. The metric estimate

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), except possibly for shared boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the image segments of \(T\) and \(B\) cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel the two leaves if necessary so that the image segment \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) lifts to a segment joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\). By Lemma 1.1,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Thus
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because endpoints of \(T\) map to points on \(\ell_T\), while \(Q\) has positive distance from \(\ell_T\).

Let the endpoints of \(T\) be \(a,c\in\partial M_\beta\), and write
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The two boundary arcs between \(a\) and \(c\) have flat lengths summing to \(2\beta\). Let \(D\) be the one containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t
\]
for some real \(t\) with \(|t|<\beta\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+h,1).
\]
Parametrize the quotient boundary circle of length \(2\beta\) as follows: a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\pmod{2\beta}\), and a top point \((u,1)\) has coordinate \(u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}\). This is compatible with \(G_\beta\), since \(G_\beta(u,0)=(u+\beta,1)\), whose coordinate is \(u\).

Thus the boundary-coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), this implies
\[
h\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of this congruence class has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Hence
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
Therefore, by Lemma 1.1 applied to \(T\),
\[
L=|F(T)|>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. Since \(H\) is a boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L,
\]
where \(\ell_F(H)\) denotes the Euclidean length of the image path \(F(H)\). Boundary arcs are strictly shortened, so
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge L.
\]
Combining with (1),
\[
\beta-t>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, \(D\) is a boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) passing through \(q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary estimate
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}
\]
holds: the minimum occurs when the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) to the line \(AC\) is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|,
\]
so
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

From (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

# Part II. Upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) and a realizing map.

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\),
\[
L<W.
\]
Choose a number \(\sigma\) with
\[
L<\sigma<W,
\]
and set
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L,\;W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

## 1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation

For \(j\in\mathbb Z\), define periodic boundary increments
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), and define \(c_j\) by
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j.
\]
Every three consecutive increments sum to \(W\), so every nine consecutive increments sum to \(3W=2\beta\). Hence
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left side of \(Q_j\) has \(x\)-coordinate
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus, for each fixed \(s\), the intervals
\[
[x_j(s),x_{j+1}(s)]
\]
tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{s\}\). Therefore the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\).

Split \(Q_j\) by the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), obtaining clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),
\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]
These triangles form a countable, locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

The action of \(G_\beta\) satisfies
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Hence
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) be the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since
\[
G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9},
\]
the indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Therefore \(D_j\) descends to the quotient triangle
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
and \(E_j\) descends to \(T_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has nine vertices \(v_0,\dots,v_8\) and nine faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

## 2. The target vertices

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in a horizontal plane in \(\mathbb R^3\), let \(o\) be its centroid, and let \(e_3\) be a unit vector perpendicular to that plane. For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3,\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\), both sufficiently small, and set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
For \(\eta,h\) small enough, every distance between points in different clusters
\[
\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\quad\text{and}\quad \{A_j,M_j,C_j\},\qquad i\ne j,
\]
lies in
\[
(\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4),
\]
while every distance between two points in the same cluster is \(<\lambda/2\).

Define nine target vertices:
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
Extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\). Define a quotient PL map \(\Phi\) by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j,
\]
affine on every face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

The nine target faces are:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(T_0)&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\Phi(T_1)&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],\\
\Phi(T_2)&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],&
\Phi(T_3)&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],\\
\Phi(T_4)&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\Phi(T_5)&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\Phi(T_6)&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\Phi(T_7)&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],\\
\Phi(T_8)&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

## 3. Embeddedness of the target surface

The three horizontal triangular faces are
\[
[A_0,A_1,A_2],\qquad [M_1,M_2,M_0],\qquad [C_2,C_0,C_1].
\]
The remaining six faces form three side panels:
\[
R_{01}=\Phi(T_0)\cup\Phi(T_4),
\]
\[
R_{12}=\Phi(T_3)\cup\Phi(T_7),
\]
\[
R_{20}=\Phi(T_1)\cup\Phi(T_6).
\]
These panels lie over the sides \(01\), \(12\), and \(20\) of the original equilateral triangle.

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(p_0,p_1,p_2\). For a homothetic triangle of scale \(r\),
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\},
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\}.
\]
The side \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) opposite \(k\), where \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), is given by
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\}\cap\mathcal H(r,z).
\]
Thus adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only in their common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap \mathcal H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The horizontal slices of the side panels are:
\[
R_{01}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h},z\right),
\qquad h\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
R_{12}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h},z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
and
\[
R_{20}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh,z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le h.
\]

Now check slices.

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{12}\) and \(R_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
\[
1+\eta\frac z{2h}>1,
\qquad
1-\eta\frac zh<1,
\]
so the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) occur. Their scale difference is
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0.
\]
Hence those adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the horizontal triangle \([A_0,A_1,A_2]\) meets \(R_{12}\) along \([A_1,A_2]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([A_2,A_0]\), exactly as prescribed.

At \(z=h\), the middle triangle \([M_1,M_2,M_0]\) meets \(R_{01}\) along \([M_0,M_1]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([M_2,M_0]\). The section of \(R_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), while the middle triangle has scale \(1-\eta\), so that section lies outside the middle triangle and is disjoint from it.

At \(z=2h\), the top triangle \([C_2,C_0,C_1]\) meets \(R_{01}\) along \([C_0,C_1]\) and meets \(R_{12}\) along \([C_1,C_2]\), exactly as prescribed.

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so its two triangular faces meet only along that diagonal. Therefore the nine target triangles intersect only in their prescribed common edges and vertices. Hence \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

## 4. Squeeze inequalities

We verify that every face map is a squeeze map.

First consider boundary edges. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=\lambda,
\]
and the target edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) joins two points in the same cluster, so its length is \(<\lambda/2\).

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then
\[
\ell_j=W-2\lambda.
\]
The target edge joins two different clusters, so its length is \(<\sigma+\Delta/4\). Since
\[
\sigma\le W-\Delta,
\]
we have
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider a nonboundary quotient edge
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
It is lifted by the segment \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}.
\]
A direct computation from the periodic increments gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]

Every target nonboundary edge joins points in different clusters, so its target length is
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since
\[
\sigma\ge L+\Delta,
\]
we have
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore every affine face map is a squeeze map.

## 5. The lifted realizing map

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\).

On vertices,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Hence, by affineness on each triangle,
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]
So \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\). Since \(\Phi\) is injective,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p\text{ and }q\text{ have the same image in }\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining Part I and Part II,
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Consequently the infimum of the realized set is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]
\(\square\)

Let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).
\]
A clean triangle has one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\); call this edge its boundary edge, and call the opposite vertex its apex.

We prove the following stronger statement:

\[
\boxed{\text{A number }\beta>0\text{ is realized if and only if }\beta>\sqrt3.}
\]

Hence the infimum of the realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

---

## 1. Lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

### 1.1. Cevian lengthening and a face-to-face refinement

Let
\[
\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge and \(a\) is the apex. Let \(A=f|_\tau\). For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
So every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened.

We may refine the triangulation so that it is face-to-face. Indeed, nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: if two such edges crossed, then near the crossing the interiors of the two incident triangles would contain open half-neighborhoods bounded by nonparallel lines, and those half-neighborhoods would overlap in an open sector, contradicting disjointness of interiors. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then since each joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), the supporting line meets each boundary component in a unique point, so the two edges have the same endpoints and are equal.

Thus only boundary edges can fail to be face-to-face. For each boundary edge, subdivide it at every original boundary vertex lying on it, and cone those subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge. The construction is \(G_\beta\)-equivariant. The cevian argument above shows every new nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened, while affine strict shortening of the original boundary edge implies strict shortening of every boundary subedge. Hence, after this refinement, we still have a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation on which \(f\) is a squeeze map on every triangle, and the triangulation is face-to-face.

We assume from now on that the triangulation is face-to-face.

### 1.2. The quotient Möbius band

Let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is a compact Möbius band. The orbit condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous injective map
\[
F:M\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\ne0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed, even powers translate horizontally by nonzero multiples of \(2\beta\), while odd powers translate horizontally by odd multiples of \(\beta\).

It follows that \(f\) is injective on each triangle. If \(f|_\tau\) were not injective, its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be distinct arbitrarily close points \(p,q\in\tau\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\).

Also, no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\tau\cap G_\beta^k\tau\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\tau\). Since \(f(z)=f(w)\) and \(f|_\tau\) is injective, \(z=w\), impossible for \(k\ne0\).

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles, so the quotient triangulation of \(M\) is finite. On each quotient boundary edge, \(F\) is affine with Lipschitz factor \(<1\). Therefore every nontrivial boundary subarc of \(M\) is strictly shortened in length by \(F\).

### 1.3. The leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider the interval of cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. Glue endpoints of such intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge of the quotient triangulation. The result is a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), whose points parametrize the leaves, i.e. the cevians in the quotient triangulation.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges; hence either the whole fan lies in \(U_C\), or none of it does. Thus \(U_C\) is open. It is also closed because it is a finite subcomplex. Since \(M\) is connected, \(U_C=M\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, and since it is a finite connected graph with all vertices of valence \(2\),
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is defined analogously upstairs. The same open-and-closed argument, using local finiteness to ensure closedness of unions of cells, shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The natural map
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering map.

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. If a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even deck power translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\). An odd deck power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Hence the covering has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\), the connected infinite cover is
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By the cevian-lengthening lemma, its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate line segment, so it determines an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented support lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). If \(\ell\) is a leaf, then \(G_\beta\ell\), oriented from bottom to top, maps to the same segment with the opposite orientation. Therefore, along one deck period, the support line path satisfies
\[
L(1)=L(0)^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

We need the following elementary lemma.

### 1.4. Oriented-line lemma

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) with
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]
Then there are two distinct parameters modulo \(1\) whose lines are perpendicular and intersect.

Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) and extend them to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), set
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, for the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanished. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map \(\psi:S^1\to S^1\) satisfying \(\psi(s+1/2)=-\psi(s)\) has odd degree: choosing a lift \(\theta:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) with \(\psi(s)=e^{i\theta(s)}\), we get
\[
\theta(s+1/2)-\theta(s)=(2m+1)\pi
\]
for a constant integer \(m\), and hence
\[
\theta(s+1)-\theta(s)=2(2m+1)\pi.
\]
Thus \(\deg\psi=2m+1\), odd. But \(\phi(\cdot,1/2)\) is homotopic through the maps \(\phi(\cdot,\delta)\), \(0\le\delta\le1/2\), to the constant map \(\phi(\cdot,0)\), so its degree must be \(0\). Contradiction.

Therefore \(a=b=0\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). The equation \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the equation \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence they intersect. This proves the lemma.

Applying the lemma to the support-line path, we obtain two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

### 1.5. Winding control

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf, oriented from bottom to top, with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
We claim
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]

If \(t=\beta\), then the endpoints are identified by \(G_\beta\), so their images under \(f\) coincide, contradicting the positive image length of \(J\). Similarly \(t=-\beta\) is impossible.

If \(t>\beta\), then \(J\) intersects \(G_\beta J\) in relative interiors. After translating, take \(J\) from \((0,0)\) to \((t,1)\). Then \(G_\beta J\) runs from \((t+\beta,0)\) to \((\beta,1)\), and the two segments meet at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate segment \(J\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Hence \(|t|<\beta\).

Parametrize the quotient boundary by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\). A top point \((u,1)\) corresponds to bottom coordinate \(u-\beta\). Thus the endpoints of \(J\) have boundary coordinates \(x\) and \(x+t-\beta\), so the two boundary arcs between them have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]
Replacing \(J\) by \(G_\beta J\) changes \(t\) to \(-t\).

### 1.6. Metric contradiction unless \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the leaves found above, with perpendicular intersecting support lines. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename them so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Also,
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By cevian lengthening,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Hence
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\).

Let \(a,c\in\partial M\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and write
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Replacing the lift by its \(G_\beta\)-translate if necessary, arrange that the boundary arc \(D\) from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc, so
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
By cevian lengthening,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta-t>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
or equivalently
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the image path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then the projection estimate
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}
\]
holds. Indeed, in the plane spanned by \(Q\) and the line \(AC\), place
\[
A=(-L/2,0),\qquad C=(L/2,0),\qquad Q=(s,y).
\]
Then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
=
\sqrt{(s+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(s-L/2)^2+y^2},
\]
which is minimized at \(s=0\) by symmetry and convexity, giving \(\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}\).

Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), contradiction. Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

So every realized \(\beta\) is strictly larger than \(\sqrt3\).

---

## 2. Upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Put
\[
b=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L_*=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Because \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(L_*<b\). Choose
\[
L_*<\sigma<b,
\]
and set
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_*,\,b-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

### 2.1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation

Define positive numbers \(\ell_j\) periodically by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
b-2\lambda, & j\equiv 2\pmod 3,\\
\lambda, & j\not\equiv 2\pmod 3.
\end{cases}
\]
Then
\[
\sum_{j=0}^{8}\ell_j=3b=2\beta.
\]
Let \(c_0=0\) and \(c_{j+1}=c_j+\ell_j\), so \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\). Define
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
The points \(X_j\) are strictly ordered along the bottom boundary, and the \(Y_j\) are strictly ordered along the top boundary.

Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(y\), the side \(X_jY_j\) has \(x\)-coordinate
\[
(1-y)c_j+y(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and these coordinates form a strictly increasing sequence tending to \(\pm\infty\). Hence the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\). Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), obtaining clean triangles
\[
D_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j\},
\]
\[
E_j=\operatorname{conv}\{Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}\}.
\]
This is a locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Moreover,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient Möbius band, let \(v_j\) be the boundary vertex represented by \(X_j\), with indices read modulo \(9\). The quotient has boundary edges \(v_jv_{j+1}\) and faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

### 2.2. Target vertices in \(\mathbb R^3\)

Take an equilateral triangle \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) of side length \(\sigma\) in the plane \(z=0\), centered at the origin. For concreteness, write
\[
p_0=\left(\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3},0,0\right),\quad
p_1=\left(-\frac{\sigma}{2\sqrt3},\frac{\sigma}{2},0\right),\quad
p_2=\left(-\frac{\sigma}{2\sqrt3},-\frac{\sigma}{2},0\right).
\]
For \(r>0\) and \(z\in\mathbb R\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=r p_i+z e_3,\qquad e_3=(0,0,1).
\]
Choose small \(\eta,h>0\), and set
\[
q_i^A=q_i(1,0),\qquad q_i^M=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad q_i^C=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Taking \(\eta,h\) sufficiently small, we may ensure:

1. If \(i\ne k\) and \(U,V\in\{A,M,C\}\), then
   \[
   \sigma-\Delta/4<|q_i^U-q_k^V|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
   \]
2. If \(i=k\) and \(U,V\in\{A,M,C\}\), then
   \[
   |q_i^U-q_i^V|<\lambda/2.
   \]

Define nine target boundary vertices, periodically modulo \(9\), by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0^C&q_0^M&q_0^A&
q_1^A&q_1^C&q_1^M&
q_2^M&q_2^A&q_2^C .
\end{array}
\]
Define a quotient PL map \(F\) by sending \(v_j\mapsto P_j\) and mapping each face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5})
\]
affinely to
\[
F_j=\operatorname{conv}\{P_j,P_{j+1},P_{j+5}\}.
\]

### 2.3. The quotient PL map is embedded

The horizontal faces are
\[
F_2=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0^A,q_1^A,q_2^A\},
\]
\[
F_5=\operatorname{conv}\{q_1^M,q_2^M,q_0^M\},
\]
\[
F_8=\operatorname{conv}\{q_2^C,q_0^C,q_1^C\}.
\]
The remaining six faces form three side panels:
\[
F_1\cup F_6,\qquad F_3\cup F_7,\qquad F_0\cup F_4.
\]

Let \(K(r)\) be the horizontal equilateral triangle with vertices \(r p_0,r p_1,r p_2\), and let
\[
S_{ij}(r)=[r p_i,r p_j]
\]
be its side from \(r p_i\) to \(r p_j\).

We use the following elementary coordinate fact. If \(0<r_1<r_2\), then
\[
S_{20}(r_1)\cap S_{12}(r_2)=\varnothing,
\]
\[
S_{01}(r_1)\cap S_{12}(r_2)=\varnothing,
\]
and
\[
S_{12}(r_2)\cap K(r_1)=\varnothing.
\]
Indeed, after ignoring the common scale factor \(\sigma/\sqrt3\), use
\[
p_0=(1,0),\quad p_1=(-1/2,\sqrt3/2),\quad p_2=(-1/2,-\sqrt3/2).
\]
The side \(S_{12}(r_2)\) has \(x=-r_2/2\). On \(S_{20}(r_1)\), the \(x\)-coordinate is \(r_1(-1/2+3s/2)\), \(0\le s\le1\); solving \(r_1(-1/2+3s/2)=-r_2/2\) gives \(s=(1-r_2/r_1)/3<0\). Similarly, intersection with \(S_{01}(r_1)\) would require \(s=(2+r_2/r_1)/3>1\). Finally, \(K(r_1)\) lies in the half-plane \(x\ge -r_1/2\), while \(S_{12}(r_2)\) lies on \(x=-r_2/2<-r_1/2\).

Now examine horizontal slices. For \(0<z<h\), the surface consists of the two side segments
\[
S_{20}\left(1-\eta z/h\right)
\quad\text{and}\quad
S_{12}\left(1+\eta z/(2h)\right),
\]
with the first scale smaller than the second. They are disjoint by the coordinate fact.

For \(h<z<2h\), the surface consists of
\[
S_{01}\left(1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h\right)
\quad\text{and}\quad
S_{12}\left(1+\eta z/(2h)\right).
\]
The difference of the second scale minus the first is
\[
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0,
\]
so these two segments are also disjoint.

At \(z=h\), the middle horizontal triangle is \(K(1-\eta)\), while the extra \(S_{12}\)-segment has scale \(1+\eta/2\), and is therefore disjoint from \(K(1-\eta)\). At \(z=0\) and \(z=2h\), the side panels meet the horizontal triangles exactly along the prescribed boundary sides.

Thus the nine target triangles meet only in common edges and vertices prescribed by the quotient triangulation. Hence \(F:M\to\mathbb R^3\) is a PL embedding.

### 2.4. Boundary edges are strictly shortened

For \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda.
\]

For \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), the flat boundary edge has length
\[
b-2\lambda.
\]
The target endpoints lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le b-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le b-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<b-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### 2.5. Nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened

Let
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
A lift of \(e_j\) is the segment from \(X_j\) to \(Y_j\). Its horizontal displacement is
\[
s_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta)-c_j=d_j-\beta,
\]
where
\[
d_j=\ell_j+\ell_{j+1}+\ell_{j+2}+\ell_{j+3}+\ell_{j+4}.
\]
From the periodic pattern,
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
b+2\lambda, & j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2b-\lambda, & j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(b=2\beta/3\), this gives
\[
|s_j|<\beta/3.
\]
Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length
\[
\sqrt{1+s_j^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_*.
\]

On the target side, \(P_j\) and \(P_{j+5}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+5}|>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_*\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>L_*.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Consequently, the affine map on every quotient triangle is a squeeze map.

### 2.6. Lift to \(\Sigma\) and verify orbit-injectivity

Define \(f\) on the strip vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The preceding edge-length estimates show that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle.

Because \(P_{j+9}=P_j\),
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) maps triangles affinely to triangles, this implies
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F:M\to\mathbb R^3\).

Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if their quotient classes in \(M\) are equal, which is equivalent to
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining the lower and upper bounds, the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

Let  
\[
\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1],\qquad \partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\},
\]
and let \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\); it has exactly one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\), called its boundary edge. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) which is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies
\[
f(p)=f(q)\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

We prove the stronger classification
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]

## 1. Every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by \(f\) on a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\). Since \(p\) and \(G_\beta p\) are in the same \(G_\beta\)-orbit, the orbit condition gives
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]

### 1.1. A refinement and a cevian lemma

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) and apex \(a\). Let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
Q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are lengthened, so \(Q(0),Q(1)>0\). Also
\[
Q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Hence \(Q\) is concave, and therefore
\[
Q(s)\ge (1-s)Q(0)+sQ(1)>0.
\]
Thus every cevian \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened. On the boundary edge, affineness gives a constant contraction factor \(<1\), so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened.

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) as follows. For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of \(\mathcal T_0\) lying on that edge, and cone those subintervals to the opposite apex. Local finiteness guarantees only finitely many subdivisions in each triangle. By the cevian lemma, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle. The refinement is still countable, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

After this refinement, which we call \(\mathcal T\), the triangulation is face-to-face: boundary-edge intersections have been subdivided at all boundary vertices; nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely without forcing overlap of triangle interiors; and two collinear nonboundary edges joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) with a common interior point have the same endpoints and hence coincide.

### 1.2. The quotient cellulation

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying \((0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y)\). Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

A nontrivial deck transformation moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y),
\]
so for \(k\ne0\),
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta.
\]

For every closed refined triangle \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\), the restriction \(f|_\Delta\) is injective. If not, its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\).

Consequently no closed refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if
\[
z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta,\qquad k\ne0,
\]
then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\). Since \(f(z)=f(w)\) and \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(z=w\), which would make \(w\) a fixed point of \(G_\beta^k\), impossible.

Let \(\pi:\Sigma\to M_\beta\) be the quotient map. If \(p,q\in\Delta\) and \(\pi(p)=\pi(q)\), then \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\). If \(k\ne0\), then \(p\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), contradiction. Hence \(k=0\), and \(p=q\). Thus \(\pi\) is injective on every closed refined triangle.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, so there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of refined triangles. Therefore \(\mathcal T\) descends to a finite PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\). Moreover, if two quotient cells meet, choose lifted representatives whose projections contain a common point; then some translate of one lifted cell meets the other upstairs, and because \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face, their intersection is a common face. Projecting gives the corresponding common face downstairs. Thus the quotient cellulation is regular and face-to-face.

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): subdivide the arc at boundary vertices; each nontrivial subarc lies in a boundary edge of a quotient triangle, where \(F\) has contraction factor \(<1\).

### 1.3. The leaf graph

For each quotient triangle \(\tau\), consider all cevians from its apex to points of its boundary edge. These cevians are parametrized by a closed interval \(I_\tau\). The endpoints of \(I_\tau\) correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\tau\). Whenever two quotient triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the corresponding endpoints of their intervals. The resulting finite graph is the quotient leaf graph, denoted \(\Lambda_M\).

Every nonboundary edge is an interior edge of the Möbius band, hence is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\), so \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

We show \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all quotient triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Interior points of selected triangles clearly have neighborhoods in \(U_C\). If a point lies in the interior of a selected nonboundary edge, then both adjacent triangles are selected because their intervals meet at the corresponding vertex of \(C\). If a point lies in the interior of a selected boundary edge, it has a one-sided neighborhood in the selected triangle. At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan; consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes, so all incident triangles lie in the same component \(C\). Thus \(U_C\) is open. The same argument applies to the complement, so \(U_C\) is also closed. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Hence
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Define the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) similarly upstairs. The same open-closed argument, using local finiteness to ensure finite vertex fans, shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The quotient map induces a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

The deck action on lifted leaves is free. A lifted leaf has endpoints \(b=(x_0,0)\) and \(t=(x_1,1)\). If an even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) fixes it, then both endpoints are translated horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so \(n=0\). If an odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) fixes it, then it must interchange the endpoints, forcing
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible because \(\beta>0\). Hence no nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf.

Thus the covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected and locally finite with every vertex of valence \(2\), it is noncompact and homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).

Orient every lifted leaf from its endpoint on \(y=0\) to its endpoint on \(y=1\). Each leaf is strictly lengthened by \(f\), so its image is a nondegenerate segment and has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented support lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). If \(\ell\) is a lifted leaf, then \(G_\beta\ell\) has the same image segment but with the opposite orientation, because \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\).

### 1.4. A lemma about oriented lines

We use the following elementary topological fact.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\), and suppose \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct parameters modulo \(1\) determine unoriented lines that are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a unit direction vector \(u\in S^2\) and its moment
\[
m=p\times u,
\]
where \(p\) is any point on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) and extend them to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
and
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and under the involution \(I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)\),
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2),
\]
so \(s\mapsto \phi(s,1/2)\) has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the cylinder \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), contradiction. Therefore \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by points \(p,q\) and directions \(u,v\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Apply the lemma to a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) joining a leaf to its \(G_\beta\)-translate. We obtain two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect. Let those support lines be \(\ell_T,\ell_B\), and let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap\ell_B.
\]

Because \(F\) is embedded, the image segments of \(T\) and \(B\) cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). Since \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) lifts to a segment joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\). Since leaves are lengthened,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Hence
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\).

Let the distinct endpoints of \(T\) be \(a,c\in\partial M_\beta\). Put
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The boundary circle of \(M_\beta\) has length \(2\beta\). Let \(D\) be the boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t
\]
for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) whose endpoints are
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Parametrize the quotient boundary circle by
\[
(u,0)\mapsto u\pmod{2\beta},\qquad (u,1)\mapsto u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
The coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is therefore \(s-\beta\pmod{2\beta}\). Since the two boundary arcs between the endpoints have lengths \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), we get
\[
s\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), this implies \(|s|\ge |t|\). Therefore
\[
L=|F(T)|>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+s^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Using boundary shortening on \(H\),
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Together with (1), this gives
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Now use the boundary arc \(D\), which passes through \(q\). Since \(Q\) is at distance \(>1\) from the line \(AC=\ell_T\),
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
For any point at distance \(y\) from a line segment of length \(L\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2},
\]
with equality when the perpendicular projection is the midpoint of \(AC\). Here \(y>1\), and by (1), \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\). Thus
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|,
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), contradiction. Hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Thus every realized \(\beta\) is strictly larger than \(\sqrt3\).

## 2. Every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad R=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(R<W\). Choose
\[
R<\sigma<W,
\]
and let
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-R,\; W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

### 2.1. A \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation

Define a \(9\)-periodic sequence of positive increments by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\) and \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\). Since every three consecutive increments sum to \(W\), every nine consecutive increments sum to \(3W=2\beta\), so
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let \(Q_j\) be the quadrilateral with vertices
\[
X_j,\ X_{j+1},\ Y_{j+1},\ Y_j.
\]
At height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Hence the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\). Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), producing clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),\qquad E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]

The action of \(G_\beta\) satisfies
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5},
\]
so
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
Thus this is a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

In the quotient, let \(v_j\) be the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(X_{j+9}=G_\beta^2(X_j)\), indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). The quotient has nine triangular faces
\[
\Theta_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

### 2.2. The target vertices

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in a horizontal plane of \(\mathbb R^3\), let \(o\) be its centroid, and let \(e_3\) be a unit vector perpendicular to that plane. For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\) sufficiently small, and set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
For \(\eta,h\) small enough, every distance between points in distinct clusters
\[
\{A_i,M_i,C_i\},\qquad \{A_j,M_j,C_j\},\quad i\ne j,
\]
lies in
\[
(\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4),
\]
and every distance between two points in the same cluster is \(<\lambda/2\).

Define nine target vertices by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
Extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\). Define a quotient PL map \(\Phi\) by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j,
\]
affine on each face \(\Theta_j\).

The nine image faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(\Theta_0)&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\Phi(\Theta_1)&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],\\
\Phi(\Theta_2)&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],&
\Phi(\Theta_3)&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],\\
\Phi(\Theta_4)&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\Phi(\Theta_5)&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\Phi(\Theta_6)&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\Phi(\Theta_7)&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],\\
\Phi(\Theta_8)&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

### 2.3. Embeddedness of the quotient surface

The faces consist of three horizontal triangles
\[
[A_0,A_1,A_2],\qquad [M_1,M_2,M_0],\qquad [C_2,C_0,C_1],
\]
and three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
R_{01}=[C_0,M_0,M_1]\cup[C_1,M_1,C_0],
\]
\[
R_{12}=[A_1,C_1,C_2]\cup[A_2,C_2,A_1],
\]
\[
R_{20}=[M_0,A_0,M_2]\cup[M_2,A_2,A_0].
\]

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(p_0,p_1,p_2\). In the plane of height \(z\), let
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}.
\]
Then
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\}.
\]
Let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) denote the side of \(\mathcal H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\). If \(r\ne s\), adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint. Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap \mathcal H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The side-panel slices are:
\[
R_{01}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h},z\right),
\qquad h\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
R_{12}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h},z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
and
\[
R_{20}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh,z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le h.
\]

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{12}\) and \(R_{20}\) occur, with scales respectively \(>1\) and \(<1\), so their adjacent side slices are disjoint. For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) occur, and
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0,
\]
so their adjacent side slices are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the lower horizontal triangle meets \(R_{12}\) along \([A_1,A_2]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([A_2,A_0]\). At \(z=h\), the middle horizontal triangle meets \(R_{01}\) along \([M_0,M_1]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([M_2,M_0]\), while \(R_{12}\) has larger scale and is disjoint from it. At \(z=2h\), the top horizontal triangle meets \(R_{01}\) along \([C_0,C_1]\) and meets \(R_{12}\) along \([C_1,C_2]\).

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so its two triangular faces meet exactly along that diagonal. Therefore the nine target triangles intersect only along their prescribed common edges and vertices. Hence \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

### 2.4. Squeeze inequalities

We verify that every face map is a squeeze map.

First consider boundary edges. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda,
\]
because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider nonboundary edges. Every nonboundary quotient edge is of the form
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
It is lifted by the segment \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}.
\]
A direct calculation from the increments gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\beta/3.
\]
Therefore
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=R.
\]

The target endpoints of every nonboundary edge lie in different clusters, so the target length is
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le\sigma-R\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>R.
\]
Hence every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Thus every affine face map is a squeeze map.

### 2.5. The realizing map on \(\Sigma\)

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over each triangle \(D_j,E_j\).

On vertices,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since both sides are affine on each triangle, \(f\circ G_\beta=f\) everywhere. Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\). Because \(\Phi\) is injective,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
\pi(p)=\pi(q)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Therefore \(\beta\) is realized.

We have shown that no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized and every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. Hence
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and in particular the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

**Claim.** A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Hence the infimum of the realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

## 1. Necessity: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

A clean triangle has exactly one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it its **boundary edge**, and call the opposite vertex its **apex**.

### 1.1. Cevian lengthening and a face-to-face refinement

Let \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the apex. Let \(A=f|_\tau\). For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
Since the two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since the boundary edge is strictly shortened and \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0.
\]
Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, so
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Therefore every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened by \(f\).

We may refine the triangulation so that it is face-to-face. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: near a transverse crossing, the two incident triangles would occupy overlapping open sectors, contradicting disjointness of interiors. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then both join \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so the supporting line determines both endpoints uniquely; hence the edges coincide. Thus only boundary edges can fail to meet face-to-face.

For each boundary edge, subdivide it at all boundary vertices lying on it, and cone the resulting subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness guarantees that each compact boundary edge is subdivided into finitely many subedges. The construction is \(G_\beta\)-equivariant. Boundary subedges are still strictly shortened, and the cevian-lengthening argument shows all new nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened. Hence, after replacing the triangulation by this refinement, we may assume the triangulation is face-to-face and \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every triangle.

### 1.2. The quotient triangulation

Let
\[
M=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
This is a compact Möbius band. The orbit condition gives \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The same orbit condition says \(F\) is injective. Since \(M\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus a nontrivial even power moves points horizontally by at least \(2\beta\), while a nontrivial odd power moves points horizontally by at least \(\beta\).

It follows that \(f|_\tau\) is injective on every triangle \(\tau\). If not, then the affine linear part of \(f|_\tau\) would have rank \(<2\), so there would be distinct arbitrarily close points \(p,q\in\tau\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would imply \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), impossible when \(|p-q|<\beta\).

Also, no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\tau\cap G_\beta^k\tau\) with \(k\neq0\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\tau\). Since \(f(z)=f(w)\) and \(f|_\tau\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the \(G_\beta\)-action.

A compact fundamental rectangle, for example \([0,2\beta]\times[0,1]\), meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness. Every triangle orbit has a representative meeting this rectangle, so the quotient triangulation of \(M\) is finite and has no self-identifications of cells.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles. Indeed, if \(e\) is a nonboundary edge and \(z\) is an interior point of \(e\), then a sufficiently small disk in \(\operatorname{int}\Sigma\) centered at \(z\) is cut by \(e\) into two open half-disks. One incident triangle occupies one side. Since the union of the triangles is all of \(\Sigma\), the other side must also be occupied by a triangle; disjointness of interiors implies that at most one triangle occupies each side. Hence exactly two triangles meet along \(e\).

Finally, every nontrivial boundary subarc of \(M\) is strictly shortened by \(F\). Indeed, on each quotient boundary edge \(e\), the map \(F\) is affine with some length factor \(\rho_e<1\). A nontrivial boundary subarc decomposes into finitely many edge-subsegments \(I_m\), so
\[
\ell_F(\gamma)=\sum_m \rho_{e_m}\ell_0(I_m)
<
\sum_m \ell_0(I_m)
=
\ell_0(\gamma).
\]

### 1.3. The leaf graph

In each quotient triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. Glue endpoints of such intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge. The resulting finite graph is denoted \(\Lambda_M\). Its points parametrize leaves, namely cevians in the quotient triangulation.

Every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) corresponds to a nonboundary edge, and every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles; hence every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\).

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles correspond to intervals meeting at the same vertex of \(\Lambda_M\), so they both lie in \(U_C\) or both lie outside it. At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, so again either the whole fan lies in \(U_C\) or none of it does. Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is also closed because it is a finite subcomplex. Since \(M\) is connected, \(U_C=M\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. A finite connected graph with every vertex of valence \(2\) is a circle:
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Define the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) analogously upstairs. The same open-and-closed argument applies; closedness now follows from local finiteness. Since \(\Sigma\) is connected, \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection \(\Sigma\to M\) induces a graph covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it fixes the leaf only if \(n=0\). An odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,
\qquad
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore the covering has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) and \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, we have
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]
Choose a parameter \(s\in\mathbb R\) on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) so that \(G_\beta\) acts by \(s\mapsto s+1\).

Orient each lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By the cevian-lengthening lemma, its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate segment, hence determines an oriented support line \(L(s)\subset\mathbb R^3\). These oriented lines vary continuously with \(s\). Since \(G_\beta\) reverses bottom and top while \(f\circ G_\beta=f\),
\[
L(s+1)=L(s)^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

### 1.4. An oriented-line lemma

We use the following elementary fact.

**Lemma.** Let \(L(s)\), \(s\in\mathbb R\), be a continuous family of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) satisfying \(L(s+1)=L(s)^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then there exist \(s\in\mathbb R\) and \(0<\delta<1\) such that \(L(s)\) and \(L(s+\delta)\) are perpendicular and intersect.

Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) with
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),
\qquad
(a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and for the involution \(I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)\),
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a map \(\mathbb S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12).
\]
Thus the circle map \(s\mapsto \phi(s,\tfrac12)\) has odd degree. Indeed, if \(\phi(s,\tfrac12)=e^{i\theta(s)}\), then
\[
\theta(s+\tfrac12)-\theta(s)=(2m+1)\pi
\]
for some fixed integer \(m\), so
\[
\theta(s+1)-\theta(s)=2(2m+1)\pi.
\]
Hence the degree is \(2m+1\), odd. But this map is homotopic through the maps \(\phi(\cdot,\delta)\), \(0\le\delta\le\frac12\), to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), so its degree must be \(0\), a contradiction.

Therefore \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). The equation \(a=0\) says the directions are perpendicular. If the two lines have representatives \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), this vanishing says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. The lemma follows.

Applying the lemma, we obtain two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

### 1.5. Winding control

Let \(J\) be a lifted leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
We claim
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]

If \(t=\beta\), then the endpoints are related by \(G_\beta\), so their \(f\)-images coincide, contradicting the fact that \(f(J)\) is a nondegenerate segment. Similarly, \(t=-\beta\) is impossible.

If \(t>\beta\), translate horizontally so that \(J\) joins \((0,0)\) to \((t,1)\). Then \(G_\beta J\), oriented from bottom to top, joins \((t+\beta,0)\) to \((\beta,1)\). The two segments intersect in their relative interiors at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\in J\) with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate segment \(J\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Hence \(|t|<\beta\).

Use the boundary coordinate \(u\in\mathbb R/2\beta\mathbb Z\) on \(\partial M\) in which \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\), while \((v,1)\) has coordinate \(v-\beta\). Thus the endpoints of \(J\) have boundary coordinates
\[
x,\qquad x+t-\beta.
\]
The two boundary arcs between them have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]
