If \(i\ne k\), \(U_i\in\mathcal C_i\), and \(V_k\in\mathcal C_k\), then \(U_iV_k\) differs from the original equilateral edge \(p_ip_k\) by a perturbation of norm at most \(2\eta R+2h\le\Delta/8\). Therefore
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}<|U_iV_k|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}. \tag{4}
\]

The cluster labels of \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) are
\[
0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2.
\]
Thus the boundary edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) lies inside one cluster when \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), and joins two different clusters when \(j\equiv2\pmod3\).

The flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), while
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\frac{\lambda}{2}<\lambda.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and by (4),
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(W=\sigma+\Delta\) and \(2\lambda\le\Delta/20\),
\[
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
So every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider nonboundary edges. Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
A direct computation from the \(3\)-periodic pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\),
\[
d_j-\beta=
\begin{cases}
-\beta/3+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
\beta/3-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Because \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Thus
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]
The other nonboundary edge in \(D_j\), namely \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), has horizontal displacement
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it also has length \(<L\). These edge types account for all nonboundary edges of the triangles \(D_j,E_j\).

Every target nonboundary edge is of the form \(P_kP_{k+5}\), up to reversing orientation, and therefore joins two different clusters. By (4),
\[
|P_kP_{k+5}|>\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(\sigma=L+\Delta\),
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}=L+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Therefore \(\Phi\) is a squeeze map on every quotient triangle.

#### 5. The lifted realizing map

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with indices on the \(P_j\)’s taken modulo \(9\). Extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous and PL.

Moreover,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) sends triangles affinely to triangles, these vertex equalities imply
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\).

Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\Longleftrightarrow
\Phi([p])=\Phi([q])
\Longleftrightarrow
[p]=[q]
\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
The squeeze inequalities above show that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\). Thus \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized, and the lower bound proved that no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized. Hence the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

**Claim.** A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Hence the set of realized numbers is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

## Proof

We prove the two directions.

---

## 1. Construction for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L_0=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(L_0<W\). Choose
\[
L_0<\sigma<W
\]
and put
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_0,\; W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

Define a positive sequence \((\ell_j)_{j\in\mathbb Z}\) by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod 3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod 3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\) and define \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\). Then
\[
c_{j+3}=c_j+W,\qquad c_{j+9}=c_j+3W=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
For \(s\in[0,1]\), the horizontal slice of \(Q_j\) at height \(s\) is the interval between
\[
(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta)
\]
and
\[
(1-s)c_{j+1}+s(c_{j+6}-\beta).
\]
Its length is \((1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0\). Hence the \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely. Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), giving clean triangles
\[
D_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j\},
\]
\[
E_j=\operatorname{conv}\{Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}\}.
\]

The triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant. Indeed,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Thus
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]

In the quotient, write \(v_j\) for the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Therefore the quotient has faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

Now choose an equilateral triangle in \(\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\subset\mathbb R^3\) with vertices \(a_0,a_1,a_2\), side length \(\sigma\), and centroid \(0\). Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\), and define
\[
q_i(r,z)=r a_i+z e_3.
\]
For small \(\eta,h>0\), assign target vertices \(P_j\in\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0(1+\eta,2h)&
q_0(1-\eta,h)&
q_0(1,0)&
q_1(1,0)&
q_1(1+\eta,2h)&
q_1(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1,0)&
q_2(1+\eta,2h).
\end{array}
\]
Choose \(\eta,h\) so small that:

1. if \(P_i,P_j\) lie in the same cluster near one \(a_k\), then
   \[
   |P_i-P_j|<\lambda/2;
   \]
2. if \(P_i,P_j\) lie in different clusters, then
   \[
   \sigma-\Delta/4<|P_i-P_j|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
   \]

Define \(F\) on the quotient by \(F(v_j)=P_j\), extending affinely over every \(T_j\).

The nine image triangles form an embedded PL Möbius band. Indeed, the triangles
\[
T_2=(v_2,v_3,v_7),\quad T_5=(v_5,v_6,v_1),\quad T_8=(v_8,v_0,v_4)
\]
map to three horizontal equilateral triangles of scales \(1\), \(1-\eta\), and \(1+\eta\), respectively. The other six triangles form three trapezoidal side panels.

Using barycentric coordinates relative to \(a_0,a_1,a_2\), the homothetic triangle
\[
\Delta(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{r a_0,r a_1,r a_2\}
\]
is described by
\[
\lambda_i\ge \frac{1-r}{3}.
\]
Its side \(S_{ij}(r)\) joining \(r a_i\) to \(r a_j\) is given by \(\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\), where \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\). Hence adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) are disjoint when \(r\ne s\), and \(S_{ij}(r)\cap\Delta(s)=\varnothing\) when \(r>s\).

The three side panels have horizontal slices
\[
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta z/h\right),\qquad 0\le z\le h,
\]
\[
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta z/(2h)\right),\qquad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
and
\[
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h\right),\qquad h\le z\le2h.
\]
The slice comparison above shows that distinct panels meet only along their prescribed common vertices or edges, and the horizontal triangles meet the panels exactly along the prescribed sides. Therefore \(F\) is injective on the quotient surface.

We now verify the squeeze inequalities.

The quotient boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has domain length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\ell_j.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Because \(\Delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

For the nonboundary edges, put
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
A direct computation from the period-three pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\beta/3.
\]
Therefore
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_0.
\]
The other nonboundary edge type \(X_{j+1}Y_j\) has the same estimate. On the target side, every nonboundary edge joins two different clusters, hence has length
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Because \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_0\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4\ge L_0+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L_0.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with \(P_{j+9}=P_j\), and extend affinely over all \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous. Also \(f\circ G_\beta=f\) on vertices and hence on every triangle. Since the induced quotient map \(F\) is embedded,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p,q\) project to the same quotient point, i.e. if and only if
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## 2. No \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. Let \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) be a realizing map for a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation. The orbit condition immediately gives
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]

We first record a useful elementary fact.

### Lemma 1: cevians are lengthened

Let \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle whose boundary edge is \([r_0,r_1]\), and let \(A:\tau\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+s r_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

Indeed, define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\left(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\right)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
So every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened. The statement about boundary subsegments follows because \(A\) restricts to an affine contraction on the boundary edge. \(\square\)

Refine the triangulation as follows. In each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the apex. Local finiteness guarantees that only finitely many subdivision points occur on each boundary edge. Performing this equivariantly gives a new locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation. By Lemma 1, \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

This refined triangulation is face-to-face. Distinct boundary edges cannot have overlapping relative interiors: at an interior point of such an overlap, both adjacent triangles would contain the same small half-disk inside \(\Sigma\), contradicting disjoint interiors. A nonboundary edge meets \(\partial\Sigma\) only at its endpoints; if such an endpoint lies in the interior of a boundary edge, it was inserted as a subdivision vertex. If two nonboundary edges meet in the interior of \(\Sigma\), then either they cross transversely or are collinear. A transverse crossing is impossible because near the crossing the two incident triangles contain open half-neighborhoods whose intersection is an open sector, again contradicting disjoint interiors. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then their supporting line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points, so the two edges have the same endpoints and coincide. Hence closed refined triangles meet only in common faces.

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The orbit condition implies that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact, \(F\) is an embedding.

The quotient triangulation is finite: a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, and every triangle has some translate meeting that rectangle.

We also need that the quotient cellulation is regular. Every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed, for \(k=2n\ne0\),
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and for \(k=2n+1\),
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus \(|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta\) for \(k\ne0\).

If \(f\) were not injective on some refined triangle, then, since \(f\) is affine there, its linear part would have a nontrivial kernel, giving arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\) in that triangle with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) with \(k\ne0\), contradicting the distance bound. Therefore \(f\) is injective on each refined triangle. Consequently no closed refined triangle, edge, or leaf has a self-identification under the deck action. The quotient is therefore a finite regular PL triangulation of \(M_\beta\).

Moreover, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): subdivide the arc at boundary vertices and endpoints; on each nontrivial subarc inside one boundary edge, \(F\) is an affine contraction.

Now construct the leaf graph. In each clean triangle, consider the interval of cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. Glue endpoints of these intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge. The resulting finite graph \(\Lambda_M\) parametrizes all leaves in \(M_\beta\).

Every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\), because every nonboundary edge of the regular triangulation is incident to exactly two triangles. The graph is connected. Indeed, if \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, both adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges, so they also all lie in the same \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is closed because the triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Thus \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\).

The lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected by the same open-closed argument, using local finiteness to ensure closedness. The projection
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering. It is infinite: no nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) fixes a lifted leaf. If a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) fixes it only if \(2n\beta=0\), while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is the infinite cyclic cover of \(\Lambda_M\), so
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, each leaf is strictly lengthened by \(f\), so its image is a nondegenerate segment with an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). Along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from a leaf \(L\) to \(G_\beta L\), the final oriented image line is the initial one with the opposite orientation, because \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components while \(f\circ G_\beta=f\).

We use the following topological lemma.

### Lemma 2: an oriented-line antipodal path contains perpendicular intersecting lines

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\), and suppose \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist distinct parameters \(s,t\) such that the underlying unoriented lines of \(L_s\) and \(L_t\) are perpendicular and intersect.

Represent an oriented affine line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and \(m=p\times u\) for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Extend the path by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
we have
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). Put
\[
\gamma(s)=\phi(s,1/2).
\]
The symmetry gives
\[
\gamma(s+1/2)=-\gamma(s).
\]
Choose a continuous angle lift \(\theta\) on \([0,1]\), so \(\gamma(s)=e^{i\theta(s)}\). Then
\[
\theta(s+1/2)-\theta(s)=(2m+1)\pi
\]
for a locally constant, hence constant, integer \(m\). Therefore
\[
\theta(1)-\theta(0)=2(2m+1)\pi,
\]
so \(\deg(\gamma)=2m+1\), which is odd. But \(\gamma\) is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant loop \(\phi(s,0)=(1,0)\), so its degree must be \(0\). Contradiction.

Thus \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere. At such a point, \(a=0\) means the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Therefore they intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 2 to the support-line path gives two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect. Let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap \ell_B.
\]

The relative interiors of distinct leaves are disjoint in \(M_\beta\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\). Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Also
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
A lifted leaf joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and by Lemma 1 its image length is strictly larger. Hence
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the image segment \(F(T)\), and put
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
The endpoints of \(T\) are distinct in the quotient: otherwise two distinct points of one refined triangle would be deck-equivalent, contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on that triangle. Therefore the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\) have positive lengths summing to \(2\beta\). Write those lengths as
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t,
\]
with \(|t|<\beta\). Let \(D\) be the arc containing \(q\), and \(H\) the other arc.

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+h,1).
\]
The boundary circle has length \(2\beta\), with arclength coordinate
\[
\rho(u,0)=u\pmod{2\beta},\qquad \rho(u,1)=u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Thus the coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two unoriented boundary arc lengths are \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\), this difference is congruent to either \(\beta-t\) or \(\beta+t\) modulo \(2\beta\). Hence
\[
h\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of the class \(\pm t\pmod{2\beta}\) has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Therefore
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
The leaf \(T\) is strictly lengthened, so
\[
L>\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. The arc \(H\) connects the endpoints of \(T\), so
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(H))\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
But \(H\) is strictly shortened:
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(H))<\beta-t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, the arc \(D\) connects the endpoints of \(T\) and passes through \(q\), so
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(D))\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line through \(A,C\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Because \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Thus every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), while every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. Therefore the realized set is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

**Claim.** A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Consequently the set of realized numbers is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

## Proof

Throughout, “decreases” and “increases” mean strictly decreases and strictly increases.

### 1. Construction for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Put
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L_0=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Then \(L_0<W\). Choose
\[
L_0<\sigma<W
\]
and set
\[
\delta=\min\{\sigma-L_0,\; W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

Define a positive bi-infinite sequence \((\ell_j)_{j\in\mathbb Z}\) by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod 3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod 3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\) and \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\). Then
\[
c_{j+3}=c_j+W,\qquad c_{j+9}=c_j+3W=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(s\in[0,1]\), the horizontal slice of \(Q_j\) is the interval between
\[
(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta)
\]
and
\[
(1-s)c_{j+1}+s(c_{j+6}-\beta),
\]
whose length is
\[
(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
These intervals tile each horizontal line end-to-end, so the \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely. Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), obtaining clean triangles
\[
D_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j\},
\]
\[
E_j=\operatorname{conv}\{Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}\}.
\]

The triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant. Indeed,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Thus
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]

In the quotient, write \(v_j\) for the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). The quotient faces are therefore
\[
\Theta_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

Now choose an equilateral triangle in \(\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\subset\mathbb R^3\) with vertices \(a_0,a_1,a_2\), side length \(\sigma\), and centroid \(0\). Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\), and write
\[
q_i(r,z)=r a_i+z e_3.
\]
For sufficiently small \(\eta,h>0\), define points \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0(1+\eta,2h)&
q_0(1-\eta,h)&
q_0(1,0)&
q_1(1,0)&
q_1(1+\eta,2h)&
q_1(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1,0)&
q_2(1+\eta,2h).
\end{array}
\]
Take indices modulo \(9\). As \(\eta,h\to0\), points in the same cluster near some \(a_i\) approach one another, while points in different clusters approach points at distance \(\sigma\). Since only finitely many pairs are involved, choose \(\eta,h\) so small that:

1. if \(P_i,P_j\) lie in the same cluster, then
   \[
   |P_i-P_j|<\lambda/2;
   \]
2. if \(P_i,P_j\) lie in different clusters, then
   \[
   \sigma-\delta/4<|P_i-P_j|<\sigma+\delta/4.
   \]

Define a quotient map \(F\) by
\[
F(v_j)=P_j
\]
and extend affinely over every face \(\Theta_j\).

We verify that \(F\) is embedded. For \(r>0\), let
\[
\Delta(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{r a_0,r a_1,r a_2\},
\]
and let \(S_{ij}(r)\) be the side of \(\Delta(r)\) joining \(r a_i\) to \(r a_j\). Using barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(a_0,a_1,a_2\), one has
\[
\Delta(r)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\},
\]
and \(S_{ij}(r)\) is described by \(\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\), where \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\). Hence adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) meet only when \(r=s\), in which case they meet at the common vertex \(r a_j\). Also
\[
S_{ij}(r)\cap \Delta(s)=\varnothing\qquad\text{whenever }r>s.
\]

The three horizontal image triangles are
\[
\Theta_2=\Delta(1)\times\{0\},
\]
\[
\Theta_5=\Delta(1-\eta)\times\{h\},
\]
\[
\Theta_8=\Delta(1+\eta)\times\{2h\}.
\]
The remaining six triangles form three side panels:
\[
\Theta_1\cup\Theta_6:\quad S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta z/h\right),\quad 0\le z\le h,
\]
\[
\Theta_3\cup\Theta_7:\quad S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta z/(2h)\right),\quad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
\Theta_0\cup\Theta_4:\quad S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h\right),\quad h\le z\le2h.
\]
Comparing slices at equal heights:

- the first and second panels meet only at \(z=0\), at the prescribed vertex \(P_7\);
- the first and third panels meet only at \(z=h\), at the prescribed vertex \(P_1\);
- the second and third panels meet only at \(z=2h\), at the prescribed vertex \(P_4\);
- the horizontal triangles meet the panels exactly along their prescribed common edges.

Thus distinct image triangles meet only in their prescribed common faces, so \(F\) is injective on the quotient surface.

It remains to verify the squeeze inequalities. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has domain length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\ell_j.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\delta/4.
\]
Since \(\delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\delta/4\le W-\frac{3\delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider the nonboundary edges. For the edge \(X_jY_j\), the horizontal displacement is
\[
c_{j+5}-c_j-\beta.
\]
A direct computation gives
\[
c_{j+5}-c_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|c_{j+5}-c_j-\beta|<\beta/3.
\]
For the edge \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), the horizontal displacement is
\[
c_{j+5}-c_{j+1}-\beta,
\]
and
\[
c_{j+5}-c_{j+1}=
\begin{cases}
W+\lambda,& j\equiv0,2\pmod3,\\
2W-2\lambda,& j\equiv1\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Again,
\[
|c_{j+5}-c_{j+1}-\beta|<\beta/3.
\]
Therefore every nonboundary edge has domain length strictly less than
\[
L_0=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]

On the target side, every nonboundary edge joins two different clusters, hence has length \(>\sigma-\delta/4\). Since \(\delta\le\sigma-L_0\),
\[
\sigma-\delta/4\ge L_0+\frac{3\delta}{4}>L_0.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous. Also \(f\circ G_\beta=f\) on vertices, hence on every triangle. Since the induced quotient map \(F\) is embedded,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p,q\) project to the same quotient point, equivalently if and only if
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Therefore every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

### 2. No \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized by a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation. The orbit condition immediately implies
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]

We first record a basic consequence of the squeeze condition.

**Lemma 1.** Let
\[
\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle whose boundary edge is \([r_0,r_1]\), and let \(A:\tau\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+s r_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, so
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Hence every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened. The statement about boundary subsegments follows because \(A\) restricts affinely to the boundary edge with contraction ratio \(<1\). \(\square\)

Refine the triangulation as follows. In each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the apex. Local finiteness guarantees that only finitely many such vertices occur on each boundary edge. Doing this equivariantly gives a locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation, and by Lemma 1 the map \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

The refined triangulation is face-to-face. Indeed, boundary edges cannot have overlapping relative interiors, because near an interior point of such an overlap the corresponding triangles would contain the same small half-disk in \(\Sigma\), contradicting disjointness of interiors. A nonboundary edge meets \(\partial\Sigma\) only at its endpoints; if such an endpoint lies in the interior of a boundary edge, it has been inserted as a subdivision vertex. Two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\), since then the two incident triangles would have overlapping interior sectors. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then their supporting line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points, so the two edges have the same endpoints and coincide. Thus closed refined triangles meet only in common faces.

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The orbit condition says that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact, \(F\) is an embedding.

The quotient triangulation is finite, because a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles. We also need that it is regular. For \(k\ne0\), every deck transformation moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\): if \(k=2n\ne0\), then
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and if \(k=2n+1\), then
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta.
\]
If \(f\) were not injective on some refined triangle, then, because \(f\) is affine there, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\) in that triangle with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting the distance lower bound. Hence \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle. Therefore no simplex has a self-identification in the quotient, and the quotient is a finite regular PL triangulation of \(M_\beta\).

Every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): subdivide the arc at boundary vertices and endpoints; each resulting piece lies in a boundary edge, where \(F\) is an affine contraction.

Now form the leaf graph. In each clean triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. Glue endpoints of such intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge. The result is a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\).

Every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\), because every nonboundary edge of a regular surface triangulation is incident to exactly two triangles. The graph \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Indeed, if \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges, so all incident triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is open. It is also closed, since the triangulation is finite. Because \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(U_C=M_\beta\), so \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite connected \(2\)-regular graph, and therefore
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Let \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) be the corresponding lifted leaf graph upstairs. The same open-closed argument, using local finiteness to ensure closedness, shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering. It is an infinite covering: no nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, if a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) fixes it only if \(2n\beta=0\), while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Thus
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, the image of each leaf is a nondegenerate segment, so it has an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). Along each cevian interval this support line varies continuously, and at a glued endpoint the two adjacent affine maps agree on the common nonboundary edge, with the same bottom-to-top orientation. Hence we get a continuous path of oriented affine lines along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). Since \(G_\beta\) swaps \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) while \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the oriented support line of \(G_\beta L\) is the same underlying line as that of \(L\), but with the opposite orientation.

We need the following elementary topological fact.

**Lemma 2.** Let \(\ell_s\), \(s\in\mathbb R\), be a continuous family of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(\ell_{s+1}\) is \(\ell_s\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist \(s\in\mathbb R\) and \(0<\varepsilon<1\) such that the underlying unoriented lines \(\ell_s\) and \(\ell_{s+\varepsilon}\) are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented affine line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Let \((u(s),m(s))\) represent \(\ell_s\), with
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\varepsilon\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\varepsilon)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\varepsilon),
\]
\[
b(s,\varepsilon)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\varepsilon)+u(s+\varepsilon)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution
\[
I(s,\varepsilon)=(s+\varepsilon,1-\varepsilon),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\varepsilon)=\frac{(a(s,\varepsilon),b(s,\varepsilon))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\varepsilon)^2+b(s,\varepsilon)^2}}
\]
defines a map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). Put
\[
\gamma(s)=\phi(s,1/2).
\]
The symmetry gives
\[
\gamma(s+1/2)=-\gamma(s).
\]
Choose a continuous angle lift \(\theta\) for \(\gamma\). Then
\[
\theta(s+1/2)-\theta(s)
\]
is an odd multiple of \(\pi\), independent of \(s\). Hence \(\gamma\) has odd degree. But \(\gamma\) is homotopic through \(\phi(s,\varepsilon)\), \(0\le\varepsilon\le1/2\), to the constant loop \(\phi(s,0)=(1,0)\), so its degree must be \(0\), a contradiction.

Thus \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \((s,\varepsilon)\). Since \(a(s,0)=1\) and \(a(s,1)=-1\), we have \(0<\varepsilon<1\). At such a point, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented using points \(p,q\) and directions \(u,v\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Therefore they intersect. \(\square\)

Apply Lemma 2 to the oriented support-line path on a fundamental interval of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). We obtain two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect. Let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap\ell_B.
\]

The relative interiors of distinct leaves are disjoint in \(M_\beta\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the image segments of \(T\) and \(B\) cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\). Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and let
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Moreover,
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
A lifted leaf joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and by Lemma 1 its image length is strictly larger. Hence
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]

Let \(a,c\in\partial M_\beta\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and write
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The endpoints \(a,c\) are distinct; otherwise a lift of \(T\) would have its two endpoints deck-equivalent while lying in a single refined triangle, contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on that triangle.

The boundary of \(M_\beta\) is a circle of length \(2\beta\). The two boundary arcs between \(a\) and \(c\) therefore have lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t
\]
for some \(|t|<\beta\). Let \(D\) be the arc containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other arc, so that
\[
\operatorname{length}(H)=\beta-t,\qquad \operatorname{length}(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Since \(y>1\), the point \(q\) is not equal to \(a\) or \(c\), so \(q\) lies in the relative interior of \(D\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+\xi,1).
\]
The boundary circle has arclength coordinate
\[
\rho(u,0)=u\pmod{2\beta},\qquad \rho(u,1)=u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Thus the coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
\xi-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
The unoriented boundary arc lengths between them are \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\), so
\[
\xi\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of the congruence class \(\pm t\pmod{2\beta}\) has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Therefore
\[
|\xi|\ge |t|.
\]
Since \(T\) is strictly lengthened,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+\xi^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. The arc \(H\) connects \(a\) to \(c\), so
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(H))\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
But \(H\) is strictly shortened, hence
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(H))<\beta-t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, the arc \(D\) connects \(a\) to \(c\) and contains \(q\), so
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(D))\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Put coordinates so that
\[
A=(-L/2,0,0),\qquad C=(L/2,0,0),
\]
and write \(Q=(s,y,0)\), where \(y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1\). Then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
=
\sqrt{(s+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(s-L/2)^2+y^2}.
\]
This even convex function of \(s\) is minimized at \(s=0\), so
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge 2\sqrt{(L/2)^2+y^2}
=\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using (1) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Since \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
and therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Thus no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining the construction and the obstruction, \(\beta\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Therefore the realized set is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

We prove the exact classification:

\[
\boxed{\ \beta\text{ is realized if and only if }\beta>\sqrt3\ }.
\]

Hence the set of realized numbers is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

Throughout, lengths in \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\) are Euclidean, and lengths in the quotient \(M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\) are induced from the flat strip.

## 1. The lower bound: realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized. Write \(G=G_\beta\). Thus there are a \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) such that \(f\) is a squeeze map on each triangle and

\[
f(p)=f(q)\Longleftrightarrow p=G^k(q)
\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

### 1.1. Cevian lengthening

Let \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\), and let \(A:\tau\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For \(r_s=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define

\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r_s)|^2-|a-r_s|^2.
\]

Since the two nonboundary edges are lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Because \(A\) is affine,

\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]

since the boundary edge is shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave and positive on \([0,1]\). Therefore every cevian \([a,r_s]\) is strictly lengthened. Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened, because \(A\) restricts affinely to the boundary edge with contraction factor \(<1\).

### 1.2. A face-to-face refinement

For each original triangle \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), let \(V_\tau\) be the set of all vertices of \(\mathcal T_0\) lying on the boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\). Since \([r_0,r_1]\) is compact and \(\mathcal T_0\) is locally finite, \(V_\tau\) is finite. Subdivide \([r_0,r_1]\) at the points of \(V_\tau\), and cone those subintervals to \(a\). Doing this for every triangle produces a locally finite \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) refining \(\mathcal T_0\).

By the cevian-lengthening observation, \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

The refined triangulation is face-to-face. Boundary overlaps have been subdivided at all relevant vertices. Two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, because then the two triangles containing them would have overlapping interiors near the crossing. Two collinear nonboundary edges cannot overlap properly, because a line joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) has unique endpoints on the two boundary components; hence such edges are either disjoint, meet at an endpoint, or coincide. Thus distinct refined triangles meet, if at all, in a common face.

### 1.3. The quotient is a finite embedded PL Möbius band

The quotient

\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle
\]

is a flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying \((0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y)\). Its boundary is one circle of length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f(Gp)=f(p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map

\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]

The realization condition implies \(F\) is injective: if \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so \(p=G^k(q)\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is an embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point is moved by distance at least \(\beta\) under \(G^k\). Indeed,

\[
G^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),\qquad
G^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some refined triangle \(\Delta\), its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would then force \(p=G^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f\) is injective on every closed refined triangle.

Consequently no refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\Delta\cap G^k\Delta\) with \(k\neq0\), then \(z=G^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), hence \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), impossible because the deck action is free.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, and every triangle has some translate meeting it. Therefore \(\mathcal T/\langle G\rangle\) is a finite regular clean triangulation of \(M_\beta\), and \(F\) is affine on each quotient triangle.

Finally, \(F\) strictly shortens every nontrivial boundary arc. Indeed, subdivide any boundary arc at the finitely many boundary vertices of the quotient triangulation. Each subarc lies in a boundary edge on which \(F\) is an affine contraction, so the total image length is strictly smaller than the flat length.

### 1.4. The cevian leaf graph

For each quotient triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. The endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary edges of the triangle. Glue two such endpoints whenever they represent the same nonboundary edge. This gives a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), whose points are called leaves.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite disjoint union of circles.

It is connected. If \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, so all incident triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is closed because the quotient triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, there is only one component. Therefore

\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct the analogous leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) upstairs. The same open-closed argument, using local finiteness for closedness, shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection \(\Sigma\to M_\beta\) induces a covering

\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G^{2n}\) shifts both endpoints by \(2n\beta\), so it cannot fix the leaf unless \(n=0\). An odd power \(G^{2n+1}\) would have to swap the endpoints, forcing simultaneously

\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]

which is impossible for \(\beta>0\). Thus the covering has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) and \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, we have

\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Choose a coordinate \(s\in\mathbb R\) on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) such that \(G\) sends \(s\) to \(s+1\).

For each lifted leaf, orient it from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate segment, because every leaf is strictly lengthened. Let \(L_s\) be the oriented affine line supporting that image segment. Inside each triangle, \(s\mapsto L_s\) is continuous because endpoints vary continuously and \(f\) is affine. At a glued nonboundary edge, the limiting segment from either adjacent triangle is the same segment with the same bottom-to-top orientation. Thus \(s\mapsto L_s\) is continuous.

Because \(G\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G=f\),

\[
L_{s+1}=L_s^{\mathrm{opp}},
\]

where \(L_s^{\mathrm{opp}}\) denotes the same unoriented line with the opposite orientation.

### 1.5. A topological lemma on oriented lines

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) with \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then there exist two distinct parameters whose underlying unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and

\[
m=p\times u
\]

for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Extend the path anti-periodically by

\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]

For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define

\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]

\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]

Then

\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]

Also, under the involution \(I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)\),

\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanished. Then

\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]

would define a continuous map to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), it is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=\frac12\), the involution gives

\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12).
\]

Any circle map satisfying this antipodal half-period condition has odd degree. But it is homotopic through \(0\le\delta\le\frac12\) to the constant map on \(\delta=0\), so its degree must be \(0\), contradiction. Therefore \((a,b)=0\) for some \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then

\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]

Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Therefore they intersect.

Applying the lemma to the path \(L_s\), we obtain two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect at some point \(P\).

### 1.6. The metric estimate

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\). Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. After possibly interchanging \(T\) and \(B\), assume \(P\) is not in the relative interior of \(F(B)\). Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). Since \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),

\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]

Because \(F(B)\) lies in a ray from \(P\),

\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]

The flat length of \(B\) is at least \(1\), because it connects the two boundary components of the strip, and \(F\) strictly lengthens \(B\). Hence

\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]

Let \(a,c\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and write

\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]

The endpoints \(a,c\) are distinct on the boundary circle; otherwise a lift of them would be deck-equivalent and their \(f\)-images would coincide, contradicting the strict lengthening of the leaf \(T\).

Let \(D\) be the boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Their flat lengths sum to \(2\beta\), so for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\),

\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with bottom endpoint \((x,0)\) and top endpoint \((x+h_T,1)\). The boundary of \(M_\beta\) can be identified isometrically with \(\mathbb R/2\beta\mathbb Z\) by assigning coordinate \(u\) to \((u,0)\) and coordinate \(u-\beta\) to \((u,1)\). Thus the coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is

\[
h_T-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]

Since the two boundary arc lengths between them are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), we get

\[
h_T\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]

Because \(|t|<\beta\), this implies

\[
|h_T|\ge |t|.
\]

Therefore, since \(T\) is strictly lengthened,

\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h_T^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. Since \(H\) joins \(a\) to \(c\),

\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]

Using (1),

\[
\beta-t>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]

so

\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, \(F(D)\) is a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\). Hence

\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]

Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the broken-line length is minimized when the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) to \(AC\) is the midpoint of \(AC\). Thus

\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]

Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),

\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]

Because \(D\) is strictly shortened,

\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]

so

\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),

\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]

For every real \(t\),

\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]

Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), impossible. Therefore

\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

So no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized.

## 2. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct a realizing map.

Set

\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad
\rho=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]

Because \(\beta>\sqrt3\),

\[
\rho<W.
\]

Define

\[
\sigma=\frac{W+\rho}{2},\qquad
\Delta=\frac{W-\rho}{2}>0.
\]

Thus

\[
\sigma=\rho+\Delta=W-\Delta.
\]

Choose

\[
\lambda=\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{40},\frac{\beta}{24}\right\}.
\]

### 2.1. A \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation

Define a \(3\)-periodic sequence \((\ell_j)_{j\in\mathbb Z}\) by

\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]

Then every three consecutive \(\ell_j\)'s sum to \(W\), and every nine consecutive \(\ell_j\)'s sum to \(3W=2\beta\).

Let \(c_0=0\) and define

\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j.
\]

Then

\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices

\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad
Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]

Let \(Q_j\) be the quadrilateral with vertices

\[
X_j,\ X_{j+1},\ Y_{j+1},\ Y_j.
\]

At height \(\theta\in[0,1]\), its left and right sides have \(x\)-coordinates

\[
x_j(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_j+\theta(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]

\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_{j+1}+\theta(c_{j+6}-\beta),
\]

and

\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)-x_j(\theta)
=(1-\theta)\ell_j+\theta\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]

Thus, at every height, the intervals \([x_j(\theta),x_{j+1}(\theta)]\) tile the horizontal line. Hence the \(Q_j\)'s tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split each \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), and define

\[
D_j=[X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j],
\qquad
E_j=[Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}].
\]

Then

\[
\mathcal T=\{D_j,E_j:j\in\mathbb Z\}
\]

is a clean locally finite triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Now

\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]

because \(Y_{j+4}=(c_{j+9}-\beta,1)=(c_j+\beta,1)\), and

\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]

Therefore

\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]

So \(\mathcal T\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient \(M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), let \(v_j\) be the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), the indices \(j\) are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient triangulation has nine triangular faces

\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],
\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

### 2.2. Target vertices in \(\mathbb R^3\)

Choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\subset\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). Let

\[
R=|p_i-o|=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3}.
\]

Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). Choose positive numbers \(\eta,h\) such that

\[
\eta R\le \frac{\lambda}{16},\qquad
h\le \frac{\lambda}{16},
\]

and

\[
\eta R\le \frac{\Delta}{32},\qquad
h\le \frac{\Delta}{32},
\]

with \(0<\eta<1\). For instance,

\[
\eta=\frac12\min\left\{\frac12,\frac{\lambda}{8R},\frac{\Delta}{16R}\right\},
\qquad
h=\frac12\min\left\{\frac{\lambda}{8},\frac{\Delta}{16}\right\}.
\]

For \(r>0\), define

\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]

Set

\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad
M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad
C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]

Define nine target vertices \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) by

\[
(P_0,\dots,P_8)
=
(C_0,M_0,A_0,A_1,C_1,M_1,M_2,A_2,C_2),
\]

and extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map

\[
\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3
\]

by

\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j,
\]

extending affinely over each face \(T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}]\).

The nine image faces are

\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\mathcal F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
\mathcal F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
\mathcal F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
\mathcal F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\mathcal F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\mathcal F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\mathcal F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
\mathcal F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

### 2.3. Embeddedness of the target Möbius band

Let

\[
H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}
\]

be the horizontal triangle of scale \(r\) at height \(z\). Let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) be the side of \(H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\).

Use barycentric coordinates \((\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\) with respect to the original equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\). If

\[
a(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]

then

\[
H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge a(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\}.
\]

If \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), then

\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=H(r,z)\cap\{\lambda_k=a(r)\}.
\]

It follows that adjacent side segments \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint when \(r\neq s\), and meet only at their common endpoint when \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then

\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The three horizontal faces are

\[
\mathcal F_2=H(1,0),\qquad
\mathcal F_5=H(1-\eta,h),\qquad
\mathcal F_8=H(1+\eta,2h).
\]

The remaining six faces form three planar convex trapezoidal panels:

\[
\Pi_{01}=\mathcal F_0\cup\mathcal F_4,
\qquad
\Pi_{12}=\mathcal F_3\cup\mathcal F_7,
\qquad
\Pi_{20}=\mathcal F_1\cup\mathcal F_6.
\]

Their horizontal slices are

\[
\Pi_{01}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad h\le \zeta\le2h,
\]

\[
\Pi_{12}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le2h,
\]

and

\[
\Pi_{20}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le h.
\]

For \(0<\zeta<h\), only \(\Pi_{12}\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) occur. Their scales are respectively \(>1\) and \(<1\), so their adjacent side slices are disjoint.

For \(h<\zeta<2h\), only \(\Pi_{01}\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) occur. Their scales differ by

\[
\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac{\zeta}{h}\right)>0,
\]

so their adjacent side slices are also disjoint.

At \(\zeta=0\), the horizontal triangle \(H(1,0)\) meets the panels exactly along the intended sides \(A_1A_2\) and \(A_2A_0\). At \(\zeta=h\), the triangle \(H(1-\eta,h)\) meets panels exactly along the intended sides \(M_0M_1\) and \(M_2M_0\); the extra slice of \(\Pi_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2>1-\eta\), hence is disjoint from \(H(1-\eta,h)\). At \(\zeta=2h\), the triangle \(H(1+\eta,2h)\) meets the panels exactly along the intended sides \(C_0C_1\) and \(C_1C_2\).

Thus the nine image triangles meet exactly according to the quotient triangulation. Hence \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

### 2.4. The squeeze inequalities

Let

\[
\mathcal C_i=\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}.
\]

If \(U_i,V_i\in\mathcal C_i\), then

\[
|U_iV_i|\le 2\eta R+2h\le \frac{\lambda}{4}<\lambda.
\]

If \(i\neq k\), \(U_i\in\mathcal C_i\), and \(V_k\in\mathcal C_k\), write

\[
U_i=p_i+\alpha_i(p_i-o)+z_i e_3,
\qquad
V_k=p_k+\alpha_k(p_k-o)+z_k e_3,
\]

with \(|\alpha_i|,|\alpha_k|\le\eta\) and \(z_i,z_k\in\{0,h,2h\}\). Then

\[
V_k-U_i=(p_k-p_i)+\alpha_k(p_k-o)-\alpha_i(p_i-o)+(z_k-z_i)e_3.
\]

The perturbation from the equilateral edge \(p_ip_k\) has norm at most

\[
2\eta R+2h\le \frac{\Delta}{8}.
\]

Therefore

\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}
<
|U_iV_k|
<
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}. \tag{4}
\]

Now consider boundary edges. The cluster labels of \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) are

\[
0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2.
\]

Thus \(P_jP_{j+1}\) lies within one cluster when \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), and joins two different clusters when \(j\equiv2\pmod3\).

The flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), while

\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\lambda.
\]

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and by (4),

\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|
<
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}
<
W-2\lambda
=
\ell_j,
\]

because \(W=\sigma+\Delta\) and \(2\lambda\le\Delta/20\). Hence every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider nonboundary edges. Let

\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]

From the \(3\)-periodic pattern,

\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]

Since \(W=2\beta/3\),

\[
d_j-\beta=
\begin{cases}
-\beta/3+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
\beta/3-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]

Because \(\lambda<\beta/12\),

\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]

Therefore

\[
|X_jY_j|
=
\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}
<
\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}
=
\rho.
\]

The other type of nonboundary edge is \(X_{j+1}Y_j\). Its horizontal displacement is

\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}
=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]

so it also has length \(<\rho\).

Every target nonboundary edge is of the form \(P_kP_{k+5}\), up to reversing orientation, and hence joins two different clusters. By (4),

\[
|P_kP_{k+5}|
>
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}
=
\rho+\frac{3\Delta}{4}
>
\rho.
\]

Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Therefore \(\Phi\) is a squeeze map on every quotient triangle.

### 2.5. Lifting to the strip

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by

\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad
f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]

with indices on the \(P_j\)'s taken modulo \(9\). Extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous and PL.

Moreover,

\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]

and

\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]

Since \(G_\beta\) maps triangles affinely to triangles, these vertex equalities imply

\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]

on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\).

Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),

\[
f(p)=f(q)
\Longleftrightarrow
\Phi([p])=\Phi([q])
\Longleftrightarrow
[p]=[q]
\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

The squeeze inequalities above show that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\). Thus every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining the lower and upper bounds,

\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\Longleftrightarrow \beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Hence the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

**Claim.** A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Hence the set of realized numbers is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

## 1. Necessity: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized. Put \(G=G_\beta\). Thus there are a \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) such that \(f\) is a squeeze map on every triangle and
\[
f(p)=f(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p=G^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

### 1.1. Refinement to a face-to-face triangulation

Let \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\), and let \(A:\tau\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For \(r_s=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r_s)|^2-|a-r_s|^2.
\]
Because the two nonboundary edges are lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave and positive on \([0,1]\). Therefore every cevian \([a,r_s]\) is strictly lengthened. Also, every subsegment of the boundary edge is strictly shortened, since \(A\) restricts to an affine contraction on that edge.

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) as follows. For each triangle \(\tau\), insert as subdivision points on its boundary edge all vertices of \(\mathcal T_0\) that lie on that boundary edge. There are only finitely many such vertices, because the boundary edge is compact and \(\mathcal T_0\) is locally finite. Cone the resulting boundary subintervals to the opposite vertex of \(\tau\). Doing this for all \(\tau\in\mathcal T_0\) gives a countable locally finite \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) refining \(\mathcal T_0\). By the cevian observation, \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

This refinement is face-to-face. Boundary-edge overlaps have been subdivided at all relevant vertices. A boundary edge and a nonboundary edge can meet only at an endpoint. Two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, because each triangle lies locally on one side of its edge, and two transverse half-planes always overlap in an open sector, contradicting disjointness of interiors. Finally, two collinear nonboundary edges either coincide or are disjoint, since a nonhorizontal line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points. Hence distinct refined triangles meet, if at all, in a common face.

Replace \(\mathcal T_0\) by this refined triangulation \(\mathcal T\).

### 1.2. The quotient triangulation

The quotient
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle
\]
is a flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying \((0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y)\). Its boundary is one circle of length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f(Gp)=f(p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The realization condition implies \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is an embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by distance at least \(\beta\) under \(G^k\). Indeed,
\[
G^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),\qquad 
G^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus \(|G^k(p)-p|\ge \beta\) for all \(k\neq0\).

Let \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\). If \(f|_\Delta\) were not injective, its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). Then \(p=G^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\), forcing \(|p-q|\ge\beta\), a contradiction. Hence \(f|_\Delta\) is injective.

Consequently, no triangle \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\) meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in \Delta\cap G^k\Delta\), then \(z=G^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Since \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the deck action.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles of \(\mathcal T\), and every triangle orbit has a representative meeting that rectangle. Therefore \(\mathcal T/\langle G\rangle\) is a finite regular clean triangulation of \(M_\beta\). On each quotient triangle, \(F\) is affine and is a squeeze map.

Also, \(F\) strictly shortens every nontrivial boundary arc. Indeed, each boundary edge of the quotient triangulation is affinely contracted, and any boundary arc is subdivided by finitely many boundary vertices into subarcs lying in boundary edges.

### 1.3. The leaf graph

For each quotient triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. The endpoints of this interval correspond to the two nonboundary edges of the triangle. Glue two such endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary edge is shared by the two adjacent triangles. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), whose points we call leaves.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence every component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

The graph \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. If \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of the quotient triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, so all incident triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Thus \(U_C\) is open. Since the quotient triangulation is finite, \(U_C\) is also closed. Because \(M_\beta\) is connected, there is only one component. Therefore
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct the analogous leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) upstairs. The same open-closed argument shows \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected; local finiteness guarantees that unions of closed triangles corresponding to components are closed. The projection \(\Sigma\to M_\beta\) induces a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to \Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\). If \(G^{2n}\) fixed it, then \(x_0+2n\beta=x_0\) and \(x_1+2n\beta=x_1\), so \(n=0\). If \(G^{2n+1}\) fixed it, then it would swap endpoints, forcing
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible because \(\beta>0\). Thus the covering has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) and \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\). Choose a coordinate \(s\in\mathbb R\) on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) such that \(G\) acts by
\[
s\mapsto s+1.
\]

For each lifted leaf, orient it from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate segment, since every leaf is strictly lengthened. Let \(L_s\) be the oriented affine line supporting the image of the leaf corresponding to \(s\). The map \(s\mapsto L_s\) is continuous, and because \(G\) swaps the boundary components while \(f\circ G=f\),
\[
L_{s+1}=L_s^{\mathrm{opp}},
\]
where \(L_s^{\mathrm{opp}}\) denotes the same unoriented line with the opposite orientation.

### 1.4. A lemma on oriented affine lines

We need the following topological fact.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) with \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then there exist two distinct parameters whose underlying unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented affine line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. This is independent of the choice of \(p\). Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Extend the path anti-periodically by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Moreover, for the involution \(I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)\),
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanished. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
would define a continuous map to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), it is constant. On \(\delta=\frac12\), the involution gives
\[
\phi\left(s+\frac12,\frac12\right)=-\phi\left(s,\frac12\right).
\]
Let \(\psi(s)=\phi(s,\frac12)\). Choose a lift \(\theta:[0,1]\to\mathbb R\) with \(\psi(s)=e^{i\theta(s)}\). The condition \(\psi(s+\frac12)=-\psi(s)\) implies
\[
\theta\left(s+\frac12\right)=\theta(s)+(2m+1)\pi
\]
for some locally constant integer \(m\), hence for a constant integer \(m\). Applying this twice gives
\[
\theta(1)-\theta(0)=2(2m+1)\pi.
\]
Thus \(\deg\psi=2m+1\), which is odd. But \(\psi\) is homotopic through the maps \(\phi(\cdot,\delta)\), \(0\le\delta\le\frac12\), to the constant map \(\phi(\cdot,0)\), so its degree must be \(0\), a contradiction. Hence \((a,b)\) vanishes somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Therefore they intersect. \(\square\)

Applying the lemma to the path \(L_s\) gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

### 1.5. The metric estimate

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\). Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. Relabel \(T,B\) if necessary so that \(P\) is not in the relative interior of \(F(B)\). Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\) and they meet at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Also \(|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|\). The flat length of \(B\) is at least \(1\), because a lift of \(B\) connects the two boundary components of \(\Sigma\), and \(F\) strictly lengthens \(B\). Therefore
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]

Let \(a,c\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and write
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The endpoints \(a,c\) are distinct on the boundary circle, because \(F(T)\) is a nondegenerate segment. Also \(q\notin\{a,c\}\), since otherwise \(Q\) would lie on \(\ell_T\), contradicting \(y>1\).

Let \(D\) be the boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Their flat lengths sum to \(2\beta\), so for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\),
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with bottom endpoint \((x,0)\) and top endpoint \((x+h_T,1)\). Then
\[
|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h_T^2}.
\]
Identify \(\partial M_\beta\) isometrically with \(\mathbb R/(2\beta\mathbb Z)\) by assigning coordinate \(u\) to \((u,0)\) and coordinate \(u-\beta\) to \((u,1)\). The coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is therefore
\[
h_T-\beta \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths between the endpoints are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), we get
\[
h_T\equiv \pm t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), every number congruent to \(\pm t\) modulo \(2\beta\) has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Hence
\[
|h_T|\ge |t|.
\]
Since \(F\) strictly lengthens \(T\),
\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h_T^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. Since \(H\) joins \(a\) to \(c\),
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Together with (1), this gives
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, \(F(D)\) is a boundary path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
The distance from \(Q\) to the line \(AC\) is \(y\). If the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) to \(AC\) has signed coordinate \(x\) from \(A\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
=\sqrt{x^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(L-x)^2+y^2}
\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Because \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Therefore \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

So every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

## 2. Sufficiency: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct an explicit realization.

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad
\rho=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(\rho<W\). Define
\[
\sigma=\frac{W+\rho}{2},\qquad
\Delta=\frac{W-\rho}{2}>0.
\]
Thus
\[
\sigma=\rho+\Delta=W-\Delta.
\]
Choose
\[
\lambda=\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{40},\frac{\beta}{24}\right\}.
\]

### 2.1. A \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation

Define a \(3\)-periodic sequence \((\ell_j)_{j\in\mathbb Z}\) by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Then every three consecutive \(\ell_j\)’s sum to \(W\), and every nine consecutive \(\ell_j\)’s sum to \(3W=2\beta\).

Let \(c_0=0\) and define
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]
Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad
Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let \(Q_j\) be the quadrilateral with vertices
\[
X_j,\ X_{j+1},\ Y_{j+1},\ Y_j.
\]
At height \(\theta\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_j+\theta(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_{j+1}+\theta(c_{j+6}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)-x_j(\theta)
=(1-\theta)\ell_j+\theta\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus, at every height, the intervals \([x_j(\theta),x_{j+1}(\theta)]\) tile the horizontal line. Hence the \(Q_j\)’s tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), and define
\[
D_j=[X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j],
\qquad
E_j=[Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}].
\]
Then
\[
\mathcal T=\{D_j,E_j:j\in\mathbb Z\}
\]
is a clean locally finite triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Moreover,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(Y_{j+4}=(c_{j+9}-\beta,1)=(c_j+\beta,1)\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad
G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So \(\mathcal T\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient \(M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), let \(v_j\) be the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(X_{j+9}=G_\beta^2(X_j)\), the indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient triangulation has nine triangular faces
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],
\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

### 2.2. Target vertices

Choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\subset\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). Let
\[
R=|p_i-o|=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3}.
\]
Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\) so small that
\[
\eta R\le \frac{\lambda}{16},\qquad h\le \frac{\lambda}{16},
\]
and
\[
\eta R\le \frac{\Delta}{32},\qquad h\le \frac{\Delta}{32}.
\]

For \(r>0\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad
M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad
C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define nine target vertices \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) by
\[
(P_0,\dots,P_8)
=
(C_0,M_0,A_0,A_1,C_1,M_1,M_2,A_2,C_2),
\]
and extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map
\[
\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3
\]
by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j,
\]
extending affinely over each face \(T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}]\).

The nine image faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\mathcal F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
\mathcal F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
\mathcal F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
\mathcal F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\mathcal F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\mathcal F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\mathcal F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
\mathcal F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

### 2.3. Embeddedness of the target Möbius band

Let
\[
H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}
\]
be the horizontal triangle of scale \(r\) at height \(z\). Let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) be the side of \(H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\).

Use barycentric coordinates \((\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\) with respect to the equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\). If
\[
a(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]
then
\[
H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge a(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\}.
\]
If \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=H(r,z)\cap\{\lambda_k=a(r)\}.
\]
It follows that two adjacent side segments \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint when \(r\neq s\), and meet only at their common endpoint when \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The three horizontal faces are
\[
\mathcal F_2=H(1,0),\qquad
\mathcal F_5=H(1-\eta,h),\qquad
\mathcal F_8=H(1+\eta,2h).
\]
The remaining six faces form three planar convex trapezoidal panels:
\[
\Pi_{01}=\mathcal F_0\cup\mathcal F_4,\qquad
\Pi_{12}=\mathcal F_3\cup\mathcal F_7,\qquad
\Pi_{20}=\mathcal F_1\cup\mathcal F_6.
\]
Their horizontal slices are
\[
\Pi_{01}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad h\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
\[
\Pi_{12}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
and
\[
\Pi_{20}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le h.
\]

Different heights are disjoint in \(\mathbb R^3\), so unintended intersections can occur only at equal height.

For \(0<\zeta<h\), only \(\Pi_{12}\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) occur. Their scales are respectively \(>1\) and \(<1\), so their adjacent side slices are disjoint.

For \(h<\zeta<2h\), only \(\Pi_{01}\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) occur. Their scales differ by
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}\right)
